front: I want to share an important update
back: Introduces a significant announcement.
example: I want to share an important update about the team structure.

front: We're making some changes to how we work
back: Introduces a process or structural change.
example: We're making some changes to how we work starting next month.

front: Here's the reasoning behind this change
back: Explains the rationale for a decision.
example: Here's the reasoning behind this change in reporting lines.

front: This change is designed to help us scale
back: Frames change as supporting growth.
example: This change is designed to help us scale more efficiently.

front: I know this might come as a surprise
back: Acknowledges unexpected news.
example: I know this might come as a surprise, so let's discuss it.

front: Let me explain what's driving this decision
back: Provides context behind a change.
example: Let me explain what's driving this decision before questions.

back: Sets expectations for a gradual transition.
example: This won't happen overnight, we'll roll it out in phases.

front: We're taking a phased approach to this rollout
back: Describes gradual implementation.
example: We're taking a phased approach to this rollout over two months.

front: This is part of a bigger strategic shift
back: Contextualizes change within larger goals.
example: This is part of a bigger strategic shift toward automation.

front: I want to be transparent about what's changing and why
back: Commits to open communication about change.
example: I want to be transparent about what's changing and why.

front: Nothing is set in stone yet
back: Notes a plan is still flexible.
example: Nothing is set in stone yet, we welcome your input.

back: Acknowledges varied impact across the team.
example: This change affects everyone differently, let's discuss individually.

front: We'll share more details as they become available
back: Sets expectations for ongoing communication.
example: We'll share more details as they become available.

front: I know change can be unsettling
back: Acknowledges discomfort with transitions.
example: I know change can be unsettling, and that's okay.

front: Your questions and concerns are welcome
back: Invites open dialogue about the change.
example: Your questions and concerns are welcome anytime.

front: We'll support you through this transition
back: Commits to helping the team adapt.
example: We'll support you through this transition every step of the way.

front: Let's address any concerns you might have
back: Opens space for worries to be discussed.
example: Let's address any concerns you might have about this.

front: This is an adjustment period for all of us
back: Normalizes difficulty during change.
example: This is an adjustment period for all of us, be patient.

front: I'll be available for one-on-ones if you need to talk
back: Offers personal support during change.
example: I'll be available for one-on-ones if you need to talk.

back: Frames change as a shared experience.

front: Let's give this new process a fair chance
back: Encourages patience with new systems.
example: Let's give this new process a fair chance before judging it.

front: I appreciate everyone's patience during this time
back: Thanks the team for tolerance during change.
example: I appreciate everyone's patience during this time of transition.

front: We'll adjust based on your feedback
back: Commits to iterating based on team input.

front: Let's focus on what stays the same, not just what's changing
back: Provides reassurance through stability.
example: Let's focus on what stays the same, not just what's changing.

back: The simulation of human intelligence processes by computer systems.
example: Artificial intelligence powers the recommendation engine on this site.

back: A subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data without explicit programming.
example: Machine learning helps the model improve its predictions over time.

back: A machine learning technique using multi-layered neural networks.
example: Deep learning powers the image recognition in this app.

back: A computing system inspired by the human brain's network of neurons.
example: The neural network was trained on thousands of labeled images.

back: The dataset used to teach a machine learning model.
example: We collected more training data to improve the model's accuracy.

back: A mathematical representation trained to make predictions from data.
example: The model predicts customer churn based on usage patterns.

back: A set of rules a machine learning system uses to learn from data.
example: We chose a decision tree algorithm for this classification task.

back: A machine learning approach using labeled data to train a model.
example: Supervised learning taught the model to recognize spam using labeled emails.

back: A machine learning approach finding patterns in data without labels.
example: Unsupervised learning grouped customers into segments automatically.

back: A learning approach where an agent learns by receiving rewards or penalties.
example: Reinforcement learning trained the agent to win the game through trial and error.

back: When a model learns training data too precisely and performs poorly on new data.
example: The model showed signs of overfitting on the training set.

back: When a model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern in the data.
example: Underfitting caused poor accuracy on both training and test data.

back: An individual measurable property used as input for a machine learning model.
example: Age and income are two features used in this prediction model.

back: The correct output or answer used to train a supervised model.
example: Each image in the dataset has a label indicating what it shows.

back: A collection of data used for training or testing a model.
example: We split the dataset into training and testing sets.

back: A metric measuring how often a model's predictions are correct.
example: The model achieved 92% accuracy on the test set.

back: A metric measuring how many predicted positives were actually correct.
example: High precision means few false positives in our spam detector.

back: A metric measuring how many actual positives were correctly identified.
example: We improved recall so fewer real spam emails slip through.

back: A field of AI focused on understanding and generating human language.
example: NLP powers the chatbot's ability to understand customer questions.

back: A field of AI focused on interpreting visual information from images or video.
example: Computer vision detects defective products on the assembly line.

back: An AI model trained on massive text data to understand and generate language.
example: This chatbot is powered by a large language model.

back: The input text given to an AI model to generate a response.
example: A well-written prompt produces much better AI responses.

back: Further training a pre-trained model on a specific, smaller dataset.
example: We fine-tuned the model on our company's support tickets.

back: The process of using a trained model to make predictions on new data.
example: Inference happens in real time whenever a user submits a query.

back: Systematic errors in a model's predictions due to skewed training data.
example: We audited the model for bias against certain demographic groups.

back: A configuration setting chosen before training that controls the learning process.
example: We tuned the learning rate hyperparameter to improve convergence.

back: One complete pass through the entire training dataset.
example: We trained the model for 50 epochs before it converged.

back: A measure of how far a model's predictions are from the actual values.
example: The loss function decreased steadily as training progressed.

back: An optimization algorithm used to minimize a model's loss function.
example: Gradient descent adjusts the model's weights to reduce error.

back: The process of creating useful input features for a machine learning model.
example: Feature engineering improved the model's accuracy significantly.

back: Cleaning and preparing raw data before feeding it into a model.
example: Data preprocessing removed duplicate and missing entries.

back: A portion of data reserved to evaluate a model's performance.
example: The model performed well on the test set it had never seen.

back: A dataset used to tune a model during training, separate from the test set.
example: We used the validation set to choose the best hyperparameters.

back: A task where a model predicts a discrete category for input data.
example: This is a classification problem - predicting spam or not spam.

back: A task where a model predicts a continuous numeric value.
example: We used regression to predict house prices based on features.

back: Grouping similar data points together without predefined labels.
example: Clustering revealed three distinct customer segments.

back: A program that simulates conversation with human users, often AI-powered.
example: Our chatbot handles most basic customer support questions.

back: AI systems capable of creating new content like text, images, or code.
example: Generative AI drafted the first version of this marketing copy.

back: A basic unit of text a language model processes, like a word or part of one.
example: This message uses about 50 tokens according to the model.

back: A numerical representation of data that captures its meaning for a model.
example: We use embeddings to find semantically similar documents.

back: A neural network architecture especially effective for image processing.

back: A neural network architecture suited for sequential data like text or time series.
example: A recurrent neural network predicted the next word in the sequence.

back: Reusing a pre-trained model as a starting point for a new, related task.
example: Transfer learning let us build an accurate model with less training data.

back: A decline in model performance as real-world data changes over time.
example: We monitor for model drift and retrain periodically.

back: AI techniques designed to make model decisions understandable to humans.

back: The process of tagging data with the correct answers for supervised learning.

back: Making a trained model available for use in a live application.

back: The study of moral issues surrounding the development and use of AI.
example: AI ethics guidelines shaped how we handle user data in our models.

back: Artificially generated data used to train models when real data is limited.

back: When an AI model generates confident but factually incorrect output.
example: The chatbot's hallucination invented a source that doesn't actually exist.

back: Application Programming Interface - a set of rules allowing software to communicate.
example: We use a weather API to display the forecast on our app.

back: An API following REST principles, using standard HTTP methods.
example: This REST API supports GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE requests.

back: A specific URL where an API can be accessed.
example: This endpoint returns a list of all active users.

back: A verb defining the action to perform on a resource, like GET or POST.
example: We use the POST HTTP method to create a new record.

back: A message sent to a server asking for data or an action.
example: The request includes the user's ID and an authentication token.

back: The data a server sends back after processing a request.
example: The response includes the user's profile information in JSON.

back: JavaScript Object Notation - a lightweight format for exchanging data.
example: The API returns data formatted as JSON.

back: Extensible Markup Language - a format for structuring data with tags.
example: This legacy system still exchanges data using XML.

back: A number indicating the result of an HTTP request.
example: A 404 status code means the resource wasn't found.

back: A piece of data used to verify a user's identity for API access.
example: Include the authentication token in the request header.

back: A unique code used to identify and authorize an application accessing an API.
example: You'll need an API key to make requests to this service.

back: Restricting the number of API requests a client can make in a given time.
example: We hit the rate limiting threshold after too many requests.

back: An automated message sent from one app to another when an event occurs.
example: The webhook notifies our system whenever a payment is completed.

back: A query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data they need.
example: GraphQL lets the frontend fetch only the fields it actually needs.

back: A protocol for exchanging structured information in web services.
example: This older banking system still uses SOAP for integrations.

back: The actual data sent in a request or response body.
example: The payload contains the new user's name and email.

back: Metadata sent along with an HTTP request or response.
example: The content-type header tells the server we're sending JSON.

back: A key-value pair added to a URL to filter or modify a request.
example: The query parameter '?page=2' fetches the second page of results.

back: The practice of managing changes to an API over time using version numbers.
example: We use API versioning so old clients aren't broken by new changes.

back: Describing an operation that produces the same result no matter how many times it's repeated.
example: A GET request should always be idempotent.

back: An open standard protocol for secure authorization between applications.
example: We use OAuth to let users log in with their Google account.

back: A server that manages and routes requests to backend services.
example: The API gateway handles authentication before forwarding requests.

back: An API exposed by an individual microservice for internal or external use.
example: Each microservice API is independently deployed and versioned.

back: An API provided by an external company or service.
example: We integrated a third-party API for payment processing.

back: A response header showing how many API requests remain in the current window.
example: Check the rate limit header to see how many requests you have left.

back: Software Development Kit - a set of tools for building on a platform or API.
example: We used the official SDK to integrate the payment service faster.

back: Converting data into a format suitable for transmission or storage.
example: Serialization converts the object into JSON before sending it.

back: Converting transmitted or stored data back into a usable object.
example: Deserialization turns the JSON response back into a usable object.

back: Reference material explaining how to use an API's endpoints.
example: The API documentation shows example requests and responses.

back: A simulated version of an API used for testing without a real backend.
example: We used a mock API to test the frontend before the backend was ready.

back: Slowing down or limiting API requests to control load.
example: API throttling kicks in when traffic exceeds our set threshold.

back: The main content returned by a server in an HTTP response.
example: The response body contains the requested user data.

back: The data sent by a client as part of an HTTP request.
example: The request body includes the new order's item list.

back: An agreement defining the expected structure of requests and responses.
example: Changing the API contract without notice broke several integrations.

back: A specification and toolset for documenting and describing REST APIs.
example: We generated interactive docs using our OpenAPI specification.

back: Splitting large sets of data across multiple pages in an API response.
example: Pagination limits each response to 20 results at a time.

back: A set duration after which a request is aborted if no response is received.
example: The request failed due to a timeout after 30 seconds.

back: Hiding complex implementation details behind a simpler API interface.
example: The API abstraction hides all the complex database logic from clients.

back: A type of access token included in requests to prove authorization.
example: Include the bearer token in the Authorization header.

back: The process of selecting the best response format based on client preferences.
example: Content negotiation lets the client choose between JSON and XML responses.

back: An error indicating too many requests were made in a given period.

back: A high-performance framework for remote procedure calls between services.
example: We use gRPC for fast internal communication between microservices.

back: An API made available for external developers to use.
example: We launched a public API so partners can integrate with our platform.

back: An API restricted for internal use within an organization.
example: This private API is only accessible from our internal network.

back: A tier defining how many API requests a customer is allowed.

back: A URL the server calls back to after completing an asynchronous process.

back: Verifying that an API behaves correctly under various conditions.
example: API testing confirmed all endpoints return the expected responses.

back: Code that processes requests before they reach the main application logic.

back: A formal agreement on the structure and format of exchanged data.

back: Marking an API version or endpoint as outdated and scheduled for removal.
example: The API deprecation notice gives developers six months to migrate.

back: Delivering computing services like storage and processing over the internet.

back: Copying data across multiple servers or locations for reliability.
example: Data replication ensures we don't lose information if one server fails.

back: Splitting a database across multiple servers to improve scalability.
example: Database sharding let us scale beyond a single server's capacity.

back: A system's ability to continue functioning despite component failures.
example: Fault tolerance kept the app running even when one region went down.

back: A documented strategy for restoring systems after a major failure.
example: Our disaster recovery plan is tested twice a year.

back: Automatically switching to a backup system when the primary fails.
example: Failover to the backup server happened within seconds of the outage.

back: The property where repeating an operation produces the same result.
example: Idempotency prevents duplicate charges if a payment request is retried.

back: A system with components on multiple networked computers working together.
example: Our distributed system spans three data centers.

back: An architecture where all components are combined into a single application.
example: The monolithic architecture made small changes require a full redeploy.

back: An architecture style structuring an application as a collection of services.
example: Service-oriented architecture let teams work on services independently.

back: A series of steps that move and transform data between systems.
example: The data pipeline processes logs and loads them into the warehouse.

back: Estimating future resource needs to ensure a system can handle growth.
example: Capacity planning helped us provision enough servers for the holiday sale.

back: A component limiting the overall performance of a system.
example: The database was the system bottleneck under heavy load.

back: A technique distributing data across nodes while minimizing redistribution on changes.
example: Consistent hashing minimized data movement when we added a new server.

back: A large storage repository holding raw data in its native format.
example: We store all raw event data in a data lake for later analysis.

back: Processing large volumes of data in scheduled groups rather than in real time.
example: Batch processing runs every night to generate the daily reports.

back: Processing data continuously as it arrives, in real time.
example: Stream processing lets us detect fraud within seconds of a transaction.

back: A request that can be safely retried without unintended side effects.
example: We made the payment API request idempotent to allow safe retries.

back: Continuously tracking a system's health and performance metrics.
example: System monitoring alerted us to the memory leak before it caused an outage.

back: The ability to understand a system's internal state from its outputs.
example: Good observability made it easy to trace the source of the error.

back: A visual representation of a system's components and their relationships.
example: The architecture diagram shows how each service connects to the database.

back: A formal evaluation of a proposed system design before implementation.
example: The design review flagged a potential scalability issue early.

back: The process of removing outdated data from a cache.
example: Cache invalidation ensures users don't see stale product prices.

back: The rules defining how and when data updates become visible across a system.
example: We chose a data consistency model that favors speed over strict accuracy.

back: A system's ability to recover quickly from failures or disruptions.
example: System resilience testing confirmed we could recover from a full region outage.

back: Running two identical environments to allow safe, zero-downtime deployments.
example: Blue-green architecture let us switch traffic instantly during releases.

back: Dividing a dataset into smaller, independent pieces for scalability.
example: Data partitioning by region improved query performance.

back: A decentralized system where nodes communicate directly without a central server.
example: The file-sharing app uses a peer-to-peer architecture.

back: A mechanism that slows data producers when consumers can't keep up.
example: Backpressure prevented the queue from overflowing during the traffic spike.

back: The process of ensuring software meets defined quality standards.
example: Quality assurance caught the bug before it reached production.

back: An error or flaw in software causing incorrect behavior.
example: We found a bug in the checkout process.

back: A set of steps and expected results used to verify functionality.
example: This test case checks that login fails with an incorrect password.

back: A collection of test cases grouped together for execution.
example: The full test suite takes about 20 minutes to run.

back: Testing software by hand without automation tools.
example: We did manual testing on the new UI before the automated tests were ready.

back: Using scripts and tools to run tests without human intervention.
example: Automated testing runs every time we push new code.

back: Testing to confirm new changes haven't broken existing functionality.
example: Regression testing confirmed the fix didn't break other features.

back: A quick test verifying the most critical functions work.
example: Smoke testing confirmed the app launches and the homepage loads.

back: Testing individual components of code in isolation.
example: Unit testing verifies each function behaves as expected on its own.

back: Testing how multiple components work together.
example: Integration testing confirmed the API and database communicate correctly.

back: Testing a complete workflow from start to finish, simulating real use.
example: End-to-end testing walks through the entire checkout process.

back: Testing performed by end users to confirm software meets their needs.
example: User acceptance testing gave final approval before launch.

back: A document describing a software defect and how to reproduce it.
example: The bug report included screenshots and reproduction steps.

back: To recreate the conditions that cause a bug to occur.
example: I couldn't reproduce the bug on my machine.

back: A separate setup used to run tests without affecting production.
example: We deployed the fix to the test environment first.

back: An unusual or extreme scenario that a system must handle correctly.
example: We added a test for the edge case of an empty input.

back: A measure of how much code is tested by automated tests.
example: We increased test coverage to catch more potential issues.

back: A rating of how serious a bug's impact is on the system.
example: This bug has high severity since it crashes the app.

back: A rating of how urgently a bug should be fixed.
example: The bug has low severity but high priority due to visibility.

back: A flaw in software causing it to behave incorrectly, similar to a bug.
example: The defect was traced back to an off-by-one error.

back: A document outlining the scope, approach, and schedule of testing activities.
example: The test plan covers all browsers and devices we support.

back: Unscripted testing where testers explore the app to find issues.
example: Exploratory testing uncovered an unexpected crash in the settings page.

back: Testing how a system performs under expected user load.
example: Load testing confirmed the server handles 1,000 concurrent users.

back: Testing a system's behavior under extreme or beyond-normal conditions.
example: Stress testing revealed the server crashes above 5,000 users.

back: Testing a system's speed, responsiveness, and stability under load.
example: Performance testing showed page load times were too slow.

back: Testing how easy and intuitive software is for real users.
example: Usability testing revealed users struggled to find the settings menu.

back: Testing a system to uncover vulnerabilities and weaknesses.
example: Security testing found an unprotected admin endpoint.

back: A structured set of tools and libraries supporting automated testing.
example: We built our test automation framework using Selenium.

back: Simulating parts of a system during testing to isolate what's being tested.
example: We used mocking to simulate the payment API during testing.

back: Sample data used specifically for testing purposes.
example: We generated realistic test data to simulate real customer orders.

back: The process of reviewing and prioritizing reported bugs.
example: During bug triage, we decided which issues to fix first.

back: Code written to automatically execute a test case.
example: The test script logs in and verifies the dashboard loads.

back: A quick check confirming a specific fix or feature works as expected.
example: Sanity testing confirmed the bug fix resolved the issue.

back: Testing a website or app across multiple web browsers.
example: Cross-browser testing found a layout issue only in Safari.

back: A tool that automatically creates sample data for testing.
example: The test data generator created 1,000 fake user records instantly.

back: The stages a reported bug goes through, from discovery to resolution.
example: The bug lifecycle moves from 'new' to 'in progress' to 'resolved'.

back: Writing tests before writing the code they verify.
example: Test-driven development helped catch design flaws early.

back: Running tests automatically and continuously throughout development.
example: Continuous testing runs on every code commit.

back: A test that incorrectly reports failure when the code is actually fine.
example: That test failure was a false positive caused by a timing issue.

back: A test that incorrectly passes when there's actually a real problem.
example: The false negative meant a real bug slipped through undetected.

back: How closely a test environment matches the production environment.
example: Poor test environment parity caused bugs to appear only in production.

back: Testing the edges of valid input ranges to catch off-by-one errors.
example: Boundary value testing checks inputs at the minimum and maximum allowed.

back: A dedicated session where a team focuses on finding bugs together.
example: We held a bug bash before the release to catch last-minute issues.

back: Formal approval from the quality assurance team before a release.
example: We need QA sign-off before deploying to production.

back: The proportion of test cases automated versus done manually.
example: We increased test automation coverage to reduce manual testing time.

back: A metric measuring the number of defects relative to code size.
example: Defect density dropped after we improved our code review process.

back: A collection of tools and code that supports running automated tests.
example: The test harness sets up the database before each test runs.

back: Conditions that must be satisfied for a feature to be accepted.
example: This feature doesn't yet meet its acceptance criteria.

back: The exact sequence of actions needed to trigger a reported bug.
example: Clear bug reproduction steps helped the developer fix it quickly.

back: A simplified placeholder implementation used to isolate code during testing.
example: We used a test stub to simulate the external service's response.

back: The standard markup language used to structure content on the web.
example: This HTML file defines the structure of the homepage.

back: A stylesheet language used to describe the visual presentation of a webpage.
example: We used CSS to make the buttons rounded and blue.

back: A programming language used to add interactivity to web pages.
example: JavaScript handles the form validation on this page.

back: Document Object Model - a programming interface representing a webpage's structure.
example: The script modifies the DOM to add a new element.

back: A design approach ensuring a website works well on all screen sizes.
example: Thanks to responsive design, the site looks great on mobile too.

back: The part of an application users interact with directly.
example: The frontend team redesigned the checkout page.

back: The server-side part of an application handling logic and data.
example: The backend processes the payment and updates the database.

back: Involving both frontend and backend development.
example: She's a full stack developer, comfortable with both React and Node.

back: Software used to access and display websites.
example: The site looks different in this browser due to a rendering bug.

back: The process of generating a visual display from code.
example: Rendering the page takes longer with this many images.

back: A screen width at which a design's layout changes.
example: The menu collapses into a hamburger icon at this breakpoint.

back: A web app that loads a single HTML page and updates content dynamically.
example: This single-page application never fully reloads the browser.

back: Generating a webpage's HTML on the server before sending it to the browser.
example: Server-side rendering improves initial load performance.

back: Referring to code or processing that happens in the user's browser.
example: This validation happens client-side before the form is submitted.

back: A reusable, encapsulated custom HTML element.
example: We built a web component for the reusable navigation bar.

back: A pattern used to select and style HTML elements.
example: This CSS selector targets all buttons with the 'primary' class.

back: A CSS layout model for arranging elements in rows or columns.
example: We used flexbox to align the icons evenly across the row.

back: A CSS system for arranging elements into rows and columns.
example: The dashboard uses a grid layout for its widgets.

back: A CSS technique to apply styles based on device characteristics.
example: This media query hides the sidebar on small screens.

back: The visible area of a web page on a device screen.
example: The viewport meta tag ensures proper scaling on mobile.

back: A clickable reference that takes users to another page or section.
example: Click the hyperlink to go to the pricing page.

back: A small piece of data stored by a browser to remember information.
example: The cookie stores the user's login session.

back: A browser feature that stores data persistently on the client's device.
example: We save the user's theme preference in local storage.

back: Browser storage that persists only for the duration of a page session.
example: The form draft is saved in session storage while typing.

back: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing - a security feature controlling requests between different domains.
example: We got a CORS error trying to call the API from a different domain.

back: Designing websites usable by people with disabilities.
example: We added alt text to images to improve web accessibility.

back: HTML that describes the meaning of content, not just its appearance.
example: Using semantic HTML tags like <article> improves accessibility and SEO.

back: A web application that offers app-like features such as offline use.
example: Our progressive web app works offline and can be installed on phones.

back: A tool that combines multiple code files into optimized bundles.
example: The bundler combines all our JavaScript files into one file.

back: A tool that converts code from one language version to another.
example: The transpiler converts modern JavaScript into a version older browsers support.

back: The process of removing unnecessary characters from code to reduce file size.
example: Minification reduced our CSS file size by 40%.

back: Loading content only when it's needed, to improve performance.
example: Images below the fold use lazy loading to speed up the page.

back: A protocol enabling real-time, two-way communication between browser and server.
example: We use a web socket to push live chat messages instantly.

back: A technique for sending and receiving data without reloading the page.
example: AJAX fetches new comments without refreshing the whole page.

back: A network of servers that deliver web content quickly based on location.
example: We use a Content Delivery Network to serve images faster worldwide.

back: Search Engine Optimization - practices to improve a site's visibility in search results.
example: Good SEO helped our blog rank higher on Google.

back: An HTML element providing metadata about a webpage.
example: The meta tag sets the page description shown in search results.

back: The small icon representing a website in browser tabs.
example: Don't forget to add a favicon before launch.

back: A service that stores website files and makes them accessible online.
example: We chose a reliable web hosting provider for the launch.

back: The human-readable address used to access a website.
example: We registered a new domain name for the product launch.

back: Domain Name System - translates domain names into IP addresses.
example: DNS propagation can take up to 48 hours after a change.

back: A digital certificate that enables secure, encrypted connections to a website.
example: We installed an SSL certificate to enable HTTPS.

back: A secure version of HTTP that encrypts data between browser and server.
example: All pages on our site now load over HTTPS.

back: A program that automatically browses the web to index content.
example: Search engine web crawlers index new pages within a few days.

back: A website made of fixed files that don't change per request.
example: Our documentation is hosted as a static site for speed.

back: Web content that changes based on user input or data.
example: The dashboard shows dynamic content based on your account.

back: A tool that generates HTML by combining templates with data.
example: The template engine inserts the user's name into the greeting.

back: Guidelines defining how to make web content accessible to people with disabilities.
example: We audited the site against the WCAG accessibility standard.

back: Code that adds modern functionality to older browsers that lack native support.
example: We added a polyfill so older browsers support this new feature.

back: An HTML structure that collects user input on a webpage.
example: The web form validates the email address before submission.

back: She is an honest person.
example: The choice between 'a' and 'an' depends on the SOUND of the next word, not its first letter. 'Honest' starts with a silent 'h', so it actually begins with a vowel sound - say the word aloud and you'll hear it. Whenever you're unsure, say the word out loud: if it starts with a vowel sound, use 'an'.

back: He is a university student.
example: 'University' starts with the letter 'u', but it's pronounced 'yoo-nih-VUR-sity' - and 'y' is a consonant sound. Since the sound is a consonant, it takes 'a', not 'an'. Always trust your ear over the spelling.

front: I want to become an teacher.
back: I want to become a teacher.
example: 'Teacher' clearly starts with a consonant sound ('t'), so it needs 'a'. This one is simple once you remember the rule is about sound: consonant sound = a, vowel sound = an.

back: Life is beautiful.
example: When you're making a general statement about something abstract - life, love, honesty, happiness - English usually drops the article completely. Only add 'the' when you mean one specific instance, like 'the life of a soldier'.

front: I play a cricket every day.
back: I play cricket every day.
example: Names of sports and games (cricket, football, chess) don't take any article at all - this is called the 'zero article'. Just say the sport's name directly after 'play', with nothing in between.

front: I saw an unicorn in the story.
back: I saw a unicorn in the story.
example: 'Unicorn' looks like it starts with a vowel, but it's pronounced 'YOO-nih-korn', starting with a consonant 'y' sound. Remember to listen for the sound, not just look at the letter.

back: He is an MBA graduate.
example: Abbreviations are pronounced letter by letter, so 'MBA' sounds like 'em-bee-ay', starting with a vowel sound. For abbreviations, say the letters out loud in your head before choosing 'a' or 'an'.

back: She bought a new car.
example: 'New' begins with the consonant sound 'n', so it takes 'a'. This is a straightforward one - just double-check you're not automatically reaching for 'an' out of habit.

back: Honesty is important.
example: When you're talking about a quality in general, like honesty, patience, or courage, you don't need 'the' in front of it. Save 'the' for when you mean a specific example of that quality.

front: I go to a school by bus.
back: I go to school by bus.
example: When you talk about the everyday activity or purpose of a place (going to school to study, going to bed to sleep), no article is needed. If you mean the physical building itself, like 'the school near my house', then you would add 'the'.

back: I met a one-eyed man.
example: 'One' is spelled with a vowel but pronounced 'wun', starting with the consonant sound 'w'. This is one of the trickiest exceptions in English, so it's worth memorizing on its own.

front: She is an best student in class.
back: She is the best student in class.
example: Superlative words - best, worst, tallest, most beautiful - describe something as number one in a group, and there can only be one 'best', so English always uses 'the' with them. Whenever you see a superlative form, pair it automatically with 'the'.

back: The sun rises in the east.
example: Some things are one-of-a-kind in our world - the sun, the moon, the internet, the sky - and these unique nouns always take 'the'. Think of 'the' here as saying 'the only one there is'.

front: I had a apple for breakfast.
back: I had an apple for breakfast.
example: 'Apple' clearly starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an'. This is one of the more obvious cases, but it's easy to slip up when speaking quickly.

back: This is a unique opportunity.
example: 'Unique' is pronounced 'yoo-NEEK', starting with the consonant 'y' sound, even though the spelling starts with a vowel letter. This follows the same pattern as 'university' and 'European' - remember the 'yoo' sound rule.

front: I am going to a hospital to visit a friend.
back: I am going to the hospital to visit a friend.
example: When you're referring to a specific, known place that both speaker and listener understand (your local hospital), use 'the', even without naming it directly. Compare this to 'go to hospital' in British English, which means going as a patient in general - context changes the article.

front: This is an unique opportunity for a beginner.
back: This is a unique opportunity for a beginner.
example: Again here, 'unique' begins with a consonant 'y' sound ('yoo-neek'), so it takes 'a', not 'an'. It helps to keep a short mental list of these 'yoo-' words: university, unique, European, uniform.

back: Dogs are loyal animals.
example: When you make a general statement about an entire category using a plural noun (dogs, cats, teachers), no article is used at all. Articles are for specific or singular references, not broad generalizations.

front: He plays the football on weekends.
back: He plays football on weekends.
example: As with cricket, football is a sport name and takes no article whatsoever. It can help to remember: sports = zero article, but musical instruments = 'the' (he plays the guitar).

back: I live in India.
example: Most country names are proper nouns that stand alone without any article. A few exceptions exist (the USA, the UK, the Netherlands) usually because they contain a common noun like 'states' or 'kingdom' inside the name.

front: The Mount Everest is the tallest mountain.
back: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain.
example: Individual named mountains and lakes usually skip the article (Mount Everest, Lake Victoria), while mountain RANGES and groups of lakes take 'the' (the Himalayas, the Great Lakes). It's a small but important distinction to remember.

front: I want to be a astronaut.
back: I want to be an astronaut.
example: 'Astronaut' begins with a clear vowel sound, so it needs 'an'. Reading it aloud slowly should make this feel natural.

back: This is a European country.
example: 'European' is pronounced 'yoor-oh-PEE-an', starting with the consonant 'y' sound, just like 'university' and 'unique'. These words are spelled with a vowel but sound like they start with a consonant.

front: She has the good sense of humor.
back: She has a good sense of humor.
example: When describing one quality that a person generally has, use 'a', not 'the' - 'the' would suggest there's only one possible sense of humor in the world. Use 'a' for a general trait someone possesses.

back: Africa has many countries.
example: Continent names, like country names, are proper nouns that don't need an article. Simply use the name directly, with no 'the' in front.

front: I need an advice from you.
back: I need some advice from you.
example: 'Advice' is an uncountable noun - you can't say 'an advice' or 'two advices' - so it never takes 'a' or 'an'. Instead, use words like 'some', 'a bit of', or 'a piece of' to talk about quantity.

front: He gave me an information about the job.
back: He gave me some information about the job.
example: Just like 'advice', 'information' is uncountable in English, so it can't take 'a/an' or become plural. Use 'some information' or 'a piece of information' instead.

front: The Himalayas is a mountain in Asia.
back: The Himalayas are mountains in Asia.
example: Mountain ranges like the Himalayas and the Alps are treated as plural, unlike single peaks (Mount Everest), and they always take 'the'. Keep this pattern in mind: single peak = no article, range = 'the' + plural verb.

front: I have a homework to do.
back: I have homework to do.
example: 'Homework' is an uncountable noun in English, so it never takes 'a' or a plural 's', even though it might feel like you have several separate assignments. Say 'some homework' or 'a piece of homework' if you want to show quantity.

back: He is an honorable man.
example: Like 'honest', the word 'honorable' has a silent 'h', so it begins with a vowel sound and needs 'an'. Whenever a word starts with 'hon-', check if the 'h' is silent.

back: This is an easy exam.
example: 'Easy' clearly starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an'. This is a simple, regular case with no hidden exceptions.

back: I need an umbrella today.
example: 'Umbrella' begins with a vowel sound, so 'an' is correct here. There's no silent letter trick involved - it's simply a vowel sound from the start.

front: She is an nurse at the hospital.
back: She is a nurse at the hospital.
example: 'Nurse' clearly starts with the consonant sound 'n', so it needs 'a'. This mistake often happens from typing or speaking quickly rather than a real misunderstanding of the rule.

back: He bought an old house.
example: 'Old' begins with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an'. Practice saying pairs like 'an old house' out loud until it feels automatic.

back: I met an honest lawyer.
example: As with 'honest' earlier, the 'h' here is silent, so the word actually starts with the vowel sound 'on-est'. This is one of the most common article mistakes learners make, so it's worth memorizing this specific word.

front: This is the most beautiful place I ever saw.
back: This is the most beautiful place I have ever seen.
example: This isn't about articles, but about a common partner mistake: after 'ever' in this structure, English needs the present perfect tense ('have seen'), not the simple past ('saw'). Whenever you use 'the most... I ever...', follow it with 'have + past participle'.

front: He is an CEO of the company.
back: He is the CEO of the company.
example: A title that only one person can hold at a time within an organization (the CEO, the president, the manager) takes 'the', because it points to one specific, unique role. Use 'a/an' only if there could be more than one, like 'a manager' in a company with several managers.

front: I want to visit a Taj Mahal.
back: I want to visit the Taj Mahal.
example: Famous, unique landmarks (the Taj Mahal, the Eiffel Tower, the Great Wall) are treated like one-of-a-kind objects, so they take 'the'. Think of these as 'named unique places', similar to the sun or the moon.

front: The moon revolves around a earth.
back: The moon revolves around the earth.
example: 'The earth' is a unique object we all share, so like the sun and the moon, it always takes 'the'. Also note: 'earth' begins with a vowel sound, so if you ever needed 'a/an' with it, it would be 'an', never 'a'.

back: She plays the piano beautifully.
example: When talking about playing a musical instrument, English traditionally uses 'the' (the piano, the guitar, the violin). This is different from sports, which take no article at all - instruments and sports follow opposite patterns.

front: He is an tallest boy in class.
back: He is the tallest boy in class.
example: As with all superlatives, 'tallest' must be paired with 'the', since it describes the single highest-ranking person in the group. Never use 'a/an' directly before a superlative.

back: This is the only solution.
example: 'Only' works like a superlative here - it means there is exactly one solution and no other - so it always takes 'the'. Words like 'only', 'same', and 'first' commonly pair with 'the' for this reason.

front: He is a most talented singer I know.
back: He is the most talented singer I know.
example: Whenever 'most' is used to form a superlative (the most talented, the most interesting), it needs 'the' in front, not 'a'. Only use 'a/an most' when 'most' means 'very' in a different, more formal sense (a most unusual event).

front: I need an hour to finish a homework.
back: I need an hour to finish the homework.
example: 'Hour' starts with a silent 'h', so it correctly takes 'an' - but 'homework' is uncountable, so if you mean a specific assignment already mentioned, use 'the homework', not 'a homework'. Two different article rules are combined in this one sentence.

back: This is a historical event.
example: In modern English, we pronounce the 'h' in 'historical' clearly, so it takes 'a', not 'an' (older or more formal writing sometimes used 'an historical', but this is now considered outdated). Trust modern everyday pronunciation over old-fashioned writing habits.

front: She is going to a USA for study.
back: She is going to the USA for study.
example: Country names that are abbreviations, or that include a common noun like 'states', 'kingdom', or 'republic', usually take 'the' (the USA, the UK). This is different from most standalone country names like India or France, which take no article.

front: The internet has changed a world.
back: The internet has changed the world.
example: 'The world' is treated as one unique, shared place, so it always takes 'the', just like the sun, the moon, and the earth. Keep a mental list of these unique, one-of-a-kind nouns that always need 'the'.

front: He is a MLA from this constituency.
back: He is an MLA from this constituency.
example: Abbreviations are judged by how their letters sound when spoken - 'MLA' is said as 'em-el-ay', which starts with a vowel sound. Always sound out an abbreviation letter by letter before deciding between 'a' and 'an'.

back: I bought an SUV yesterday.
example: 'SUV' is pronounced 'es-yoo-vee', beginning with the vowel sound 'es', so it needs 'an'. This is another abbreviation where the sound doesn't match the first letter's typical sound.

back: This is an hour-long meeting.
example: 'Hour' has a silent 'h', so it actually begins with the vowel sound 'ow-er'. This is a classic exception worth memorizing by itself, alongside 'honest' and 'honorable'.

front: i am going to the market.
back: I am going to the market.
example: The pronoun 'I' is always capitalized in English, no matter where it appears in the sentence - this is a unique rule specific to this one word. Always capitalize 'I' whether it's at the start, middle, or end of a sentence.

back: My friend is from India.
example: Two capitalization rules are missing here: the first word of a sentence must be capitalized, and proper nouns like country names (India) are always capitalized. Check both the start of every sentence and every proper noun (names of people, places, days, months) for correct capitalization.

back: She was born in March.
example: Month names are always capitalized in English, along with the first word of the sentence. Remember: days of the week and months of the year are always proper nouns, requiring capital letters.

back: We celebrate Diwali every year.
example: Names of festivals and holidays (Diwali, Christmas, Eid) are proper nouns and need capital letters. Treat festival names the same way you'd treat any other proper noun - always capitalized.

back: He speaks English and Hindi.
example: Language names are always capitalized in English, since they're derived from proper nouns (countries or regions). Remember: English, Hindi, Spanish, French - all languages get capital letters, even mid-sentence.

back: What's your name?
example: Two things are missing: the apostrophe in the contraction "what's" (what is), and the question mark at the end since this is a question. Always check contractions for their apostrophe, and questions for their question mark.

back: I'm going home now.
example: This is missing the apostrophe in "I'm" (I am) and the period at the end of the sentence. Contractions always need an apostrophe to show where letters have been removed.

back: Don't forget to call me.
example: The contraction "don't" (do not) needs an apostrophe between the n and t, and the sentence needs a capital letter at the start and a period at the end. Always place the apostrophe exactly where letters are removed - in 'don't', it replaces the missing 'o' from 'not'.

back: It's a beautiful day today.
example: "It's" (meaning 'it is') needs an apostrophe, and the sentence needs both a capital letter at the start and a period at the end. This is one of the most common punctuation mistakes, so it's worth double-checking every time you use 'its' or "it's".

front: the meeting is at 3pm on monday
back: The meeting is at 3 PM on Monday.
example: This is missing capitalization for the sentence start and the day name 'Monday', and it's good practice to add a space before 'PM' with capital letters. Days of the week always need capital letters, just like months.

back: I think, therefore, I am.
example: Beyond capitalizing 'I' both times, this famous phrase traditionally includes a comma after 'therefore' as well, to properly set off the transitional word. Transitional words like 'therefore', 'however', and 'moreover' are often set off with commas on both sides when placed mid-sentence.

back: Let's eat, Grandma!
example: This famous example shows how missing punctuation can change meaning entirely - without a comma, it sounds like you want to eat your grandmother, but with the comma, you're addressing her about eating. Commas before a name when speaking directly to someone (called direct address) are essential for clarity and can even be a matter of life and death, as this joke illustrates!

front: i have a dog a cat and a bird.
back: I have a dog, a cat, and a bird.
example: This sentence needs commas between each item in the list to separate them clearly - without commas, the items run together confusingly. Always separate three or more items in a list with commas, including before 'and' (called the Oxford comma, though its use varies by style).

front: she said 'I will be there soon'
back: She said, "I will be there soon."
example: In American English, double quotation marks are standard for direct speech (not single quotes), and a comma is needed before the quote begins, plus a period inside the closing quotation mark. Use double quotes for speech in American English, and place the closing punctuation inside the quotation marks.

front: the report is due on friday, however, i wont be able to submit it on time.
back: The report is due on Friday. However, I won't be able to submit it on time.
example: This is actually a comma splice mistake - 'however' used this way needs a period (or semicolon) before it, not just a comma, since it's joining two complete sentences. Remember: 'however' as a transition word between two full sentences needs a period or semicolon before it, not a comma alone.

back: My brother's name is Raj.
example: This needs an apostrophe to show possession ('brother's' meaning belonging to the brother) and a capital letter for the proper noun 'Raj'. Remember: apostrophe + s shows singular possession (brother's), and names always need capital letters.

front: the childrens toys were scattered everywhere.
back: The children's toys were scattered everywhere.
example: Since 'children' is already plural (without adding 's'), the possessive form simply adds apostrophe + s ('children's'), following the irregular plural possessive rule. For irregular plurals (children, men, women), add apostrophe + s, just like a singular noun would.

front: the dogs bone was buried in the yard.
back: The dog's bone was buried in the yard.
example: This needs an apostrophe before the 's' to show the bone belongs to one dog ('dog's'), following the standard singular possessive rule. Remember: for singular nouns, add apostrophe before the s to show possession.

front: the students papers were graded quickly.
back: The students' papers were graded quickly.
example: Since 'students' is already plural (ending in s), the possessive apostrophe goes AFTER the s ('students''), not before it. For regular plural nouns already ending in s, add just an apostrophe after the s, not another 's'.

front: James book is on the table.
back: James's book is on the table. / James' book is on the table.
example: For singular names ending in 's' (like James), both 'James's' and 'James'' are considered acceptable in different style guides - choose one and be consistent. Either style works, but pick one approach and use it consistently throughout your writing.

front: she asked me, "where are you going"
back: She asked me, "Where are you going?"
example: The quoted question needs its own question mark inside the quotation marks, and the first word of the quote should be capitalized since it starts a new sentence within the quote. Always capitalize the first word of a quoted sentence, and include appropriate end punctuation inside the quotes.

back: He lives on Main Street.
example: When 'Street' is part of an official place name, it should be capitalized just like the rest of the name (Main Street). Capitalize words like Street, Avenue, and Road when they're part of a specific, named location.

front: she works at the Company down the road.
back: She works at the company down the road.
example: Unless referring to a specific, named company, 'company' here is a common noun and shouldn't be capitalized. Only capitalize words like 'company', 'college', or 'university' when they're part of an official proper name (like Apple Company or Delhi University).

front: we visited the taj mahal last summer.
back: We visited the Taj Mahal last summer.
example: Famous landmark names are proper nouns and need capital letters for every important word in the name. Treat multi-word landmark and place names the same way: capitalize each significant word.

back: My mom is a doctor. / My Mom is a doctor (if used as a name/title directly).
example: When 'mom' is used generally (my mom, his mom), it's a common noun and stays lowercase - but when used directly as a name in place of 'Mom' (like 'Hi, Mom!'), it's capitalized. Check whether you're using a family term generally (lowercase) or as a direct name/title (capitalized).

back: Can you help me, please?
example: Since this sentence is a question (asking for help), it needs a question mark at the end, not a period. Always check whether a sentence is truly a question and needs a question mark, even if it sounds like a polite request.

back: That's amazing!
example: This sentence has both a period and an exclamation mark together, which is unnecessary - only one end punctuation mark is needed per sentence. Choose one final punctuation mark: period, question mark, or exclamation mark, never combine them.

back: Why did you do that?! (acceptable informally) / Why did you do that?
example: While combining '?!' is common in very casual writing to show shocked surprise, formal writing should use just one punctuation mark (usually the question mark alone). Save combined punctuation like '?!' for informal contexts; use single end punctuation in formal writing.

front: The company's, revenue increased this year.
back: The company's revenue increased this year.
example: This sentence has an unnecessary comma right after the possessive apostrophe-s - possessives don't need a comma immediately following them. Remove commas that don't serve a clear grammatical purpose, like separating necessary information.

front: My sister who lives in Mumbai, is visiting us.
back: My sister, who lives in Mumbai, is visiting us.
example: This needs a comma BEFORE 'who' as well, since 'who lives in Mumbai' is extra, non-essential information about the sister (called a non-restrictive clause) and needs commas on both sides. Non-essential descriptive clauses need commas both before and after, not just after.

front: The book, that I borrowed from the library, is due tomorrow.
back: The book that I borrowed from the library is due tomorrow.
example: Unlike the previous example, this clause ('that I borrowed from the library') is ESSENTIAL to identifying which book, so it shouldn't have commas around it at all. The key difference: essential/restrictive clauses (usually with 'that') don't get commas; non-essential/non-restrictive clauses (usually with 'which' or 'who') do.

front: Please bring your laptop, phone, and charger, to the meeting.
back: Please bring your laptop, phone, and charger to the meeting.
example: This sentence has an unnecessary comma right before 'to the meeting' - once the list is complete, no additional comma is needed before continuing the sentence. Only use commas to separate list items; don't add an extra one right after the last item before continuing the sentence.

front: Although, it was raining, we went for a walk.
back: Although it was raining, we went for a walk.
example: There shouldn't be a comma right after 'although' - the comma belongs at the end of the entire dependent clause, not right after the conjunction itself. Place the comma after the complete dependent clause ('although it was raining,'), not right after the conjunction word alone.

front: She bought, apples, oranges, and bananas.
back: She bought apples, oranges, and bananas.
example: There's an unnecessary comma right after 'bought' - commas should only separate the list items themselves, not come between the verb and the start of the list. Remove any comma that appears between a verb and its object unless there's a specific grammatical reason for it.

front: Its important to, arrive on time.
back: It's important to arrive on time.
example: This has two issues: "it's" needs an apostrophe (it is important), and there's an unnecessary comma right before 'arrive' that breaks up the infinitive phrase. Never place a comma between 'to' and the base verb in an infinitive phrase (to arrive, to go, to see).

front: The weather was cold, and, we stayed inside.
back: The weather was cold, and we stayed inside.
example: This sentence has an unnecessary extra comma right after 'and' - only one comma is needed before the conjunction joining two complete sentences. Place just one comma before the conjunction (and, but, so) when joining two complete sentences, not an extra one after it too.

front: I like reading; especially mystery novels.
back: I like reading, especially mystery novels.
example: A semicolon is too strong here since 'especially mystery novels' isn't a complete sentence on its own - use a comma instead for this kind of added detail. Semicolons should only join two complete, independent sentences; use a comma for incomplete phrases added onto a sentence.

front: I have one goal; to graduate with honors.
back: I have one goal: to graduate with honors.
example: A colon (not a semicolon) is used to introduce an explanation, example, or elaboration of what came before - here, the colon correctly introduces what the goal actually is. Remember: colons introduce/explain, semicolons join two complete related sentences - they serve different functions.

front: There are three things I need; milk, eggs, and bread.
back: There are three things I need: milk, eggs, and bread.
example: Just like the previous example, a colon (not semicolon) correctly introduces this list, since 'milk, eggs, and bread' isn't a complete sentence. Use a colon before a list that explains or elaborates on something mentioned just before it.

front: I finished the report; and I sent it to my manager.
back: I finished the report, and I sent it to my manager. / I finished the report; I sent it to my manager.
example: You shouldn't combine a semicolon with a conjunction like 'and' - choose either a comma plus 'and', or a semicolon alone without any conjunction. These are two separate, valid ways to join sentences; don't mix them together.

front: Can I ask you something; it's important.
back: Can I ask you something? It's important. / Can I ask you something - it's important.
example: Since the first part is a genuine question, it needs a question mark, not a semicolon, which is reserved for joining two complete statements (not mixing a question with a statement). Match your punctuation to the actual sentence type - questions need question marks, regardless of what follows.

front: the CEO of the company mr. smith, gave a speech.
back: The CEO of the company, Mr. Smith, gave a speech.
example: This needs the sentence-starting capital letter, a period after the abbreviation 'Mr.', capitalization of the name 'Smith', and commas around the appositive phrase (Mr. Smith) since it renames 'the CEO'. Titles like Mr., Mrs., and Dr. always need a period after the abbreviation and a capital letter.

front: dr Patel will see you now.
back: Dr. Patel will see you now.
example: The title 'Dr' needs both a period (since it's an abbreviation for 'Doctor') and a capital letter, as it's part of the person's name/title. Remember to add periods after common title abbreviations: Dr., Mr., Mrs., Ms.

back: I was born on July 4th, 1990.
example: This needs the month capitalized ('July'), and a comma between the day and the year in this date format. Remember the standard date format: Month Day, Year - with a comma separating the day and year.

front: The company's headquarters is in new york city.
back: The company's headquarters is in New York City.
example: Every word of this proper place name needs to be capitalized (New York City), following the standard rule for multi-word proper nouns. Treat city and place names the same as other proper nouns - capitalize every significant word in the full name.

front: she works for the united nations.
back: She works for the United Nations.
example: Official organization names are proper nouns, so every important word needs a capital letter (United Nations). Capitalize the full official name of organizations, companies, and institutions consistently.

front: Its raining, so bring your umbrella, ok?
back: It's raining, so bring your umbrella, okay?
example: This needs the apostrophe fixed (it's), and while 'ok' is common informally, the fuller spelling 'okay' is generally preferred in more careful writing. Use "it's" (not 'its') whenever you mean 'it is', and consider spelling out 'okay' fully in more formal contexts.

front: who's turn is it to cook dinner tonight.
back: Whose turn is it to cook dinner tonight?
example: This sentence confuses "who's" (who is) with 'whose' (possession) - since this asks about ownership of the turn, 'whose' is correct, and it also needs a question mark since it's a question. Double-check both the correct word (whose vs who's) and the correct end punctuation for genuine questions.

front: The presentation went well, the client's were impressed.
back: The presentation went well; the clients were impressed.
example: This has two fixes: use a semicolon (not comma) to join these two complete, related sentences, and remove the incorrect apostrophe from 'client's' since this is simply a plural (clients), not a possessive. Watch for accidentally adding an apostrophe to a word that's just plural, not showing ownership.

front: we're moving to Chicago in august.
back: We're moving to Chicago in August.
example: This needs the sentence-starting capital letter and the month name 'August' capitalized, since months are always proper nouns. Keep checking every sentence for these two very common capitalization spots: the first word, and any months or days mentioned.

front: She is more taller than me.
back: She is taller than me.
example: 'Taller' already includes the comparative ending '-er', so adding 'more' in front creates a double comparative, which is incorrect. For short, one-syllable adjectives, add just '-er' - never combine it with 'more'.

front: This is the most tallest building.
back: This is the tallest building.
example: Just like the comparative mistake, 'tallest' already has the superlative ending '-est', so adding 'most' in front doubles up unnecessarily. For short adjectives, use just '-est' alone for the superlative, never with 'most' added.

front: He is the most smartest student.
back: He is the smartest student.
example: 'Smartest' already carries the superlative meaning through its ending, so 'most' in front repeats the same function. Remember this general rule: never combine 'most/more' with an adjective that already ends in '-er' or '-est'.

front: This is more better than that.
back: This is better than that.
example: 'Better' is already the comparative form of 'good' (an irregular form), so adding 'more' in front is redundant. Irregular comparatives like 'better' and 'worse' never need 'more' added to them.

front: This is the most best option.
back: This is the best option.
example: 'Best' is already the superlative form of 'good', so 'most' in front repeats the same idea unnecessarily. Just like 'better', irregular superlatives like 'best' and 'worst' never take 'most'.

front: She sings more beautifuller than him.
back: She sings more beautifully than him.
example: 'Beautifully' is a longer word, so it needs 'more' in front rather than an '-er' ending directly attached - 'beautifuller' isn't a real word. Remember: longer adjectives and adverbs (usually two or more syllables) use 'more/most', while short ones use '-er/-est'.

front: This is the beautifullest place I've seen.
back: This is the most beautiful place I've seen.
example: Since 'beautiful' has three syllables, it needs 'most' in front for the superlative, not an '-est' ending directly attached. Longer words like beautiful, intelligent, and important always use 'most', never a direct '-est' ending.

front: He is more intelligenter than his brother.
back: He is more intelligent than his brother.
example: 'Intelligent' is a long word (four syllables), so it only needs 'more' added, not an additional '-er' ending on top. Remember: long words get 'more/most' alone, with no extra ending added to the word itself.

front: This road is more wide than that one.
back: This road is wider than that one.
example: 'Wide' is a short, one-syllable word, so it should take the '-er' ending directly (wider), not 'more' in front. Short adjectives (one syllable, sometimes two) take '-er/-est' endings rather than 'more/most'.

front: This is more easy than I expected.
back: This is easier than I expected.
example: 'Easy' is a two-syllable word ending in 'y', which usually takes '-er' (with the y changed to i) rather than 'more'. For two-syllable words ending in 'y' (happy, easy, busy), change the y to i and add '-er/-est'.

front: She is the most happiest person I know.
back: She is the happiest person I know.
example: 'Happiest' already contains the superlative form (with y changed to i, plus -est), so 'most' in front is unnecessary repetition. This follows the same y-to-i rule as 'easier/easiest', without needing 'most' added.

front: He is more funnier than his friend.
back: He is funnier than his friend.
example: 'Funny' follows the same two-syllable, y-ending pattern as 'happy' and 'easy' - it takes '-er' directly (funnier), not 'more'. Keep grouping these y-ending, two-syllable words together in memory since they all follow the same rule.

front: This is the most simplest solution.
back: This is the simplest solution.
example: 'Simplest' already carries the superlative meaning through its ending, making 'most' redundant. Even though 'simple' has two syllables, it's one of the exceptions that commonly takes '-er/-est' rather than 'more/most' - so no 'most' is needed here.

front: She is more prettier than her sister.
back: She is prettier than her sister.
example: 'Pretty' follows the same y-ending, two-syllable rule as happy and easy - it becomes 'prettier' directly, without needing 'more' added. Practice recognizing this specific word pattern: two syllables ending in 'y' = change to 'i' + '-er/-est'.

front: This is the goodest movie I've seen.
back: This is the best movie I've seen.
example: 'Good' is an irregular adjective - its comparative and superlative forms are completely different words (better, best), not simply 'gooder' or 'goodest'. Memorize irregular forms separately since they don't follow the normal rules: good-better-best, bad-worse-worst.

front: This is badder than I thought.
back: This is worse than I thought.
example: 'Bad' is irregular, and its comparative form is 'worse' (not 'badder'), just as 'good' becomes 'better'. Keep memorizing this irregular pair alongside good/better/best: bad-worse-worst.

front: This is the baddest experience ever.
back: This is the worst experience ever.
example: Just like 'badder', 'baddest' isn't a standard word - the correct superlative of 'bad' is 'worst'. Complete the irregular set: bad, worse, worst - none of these add a regular ending.

front: I have less money than more people think.
back: I have less money than most people think.
example: This sentence confuses 'more' (extra than) with 'most' (the majority of) - since the intended meaning is 'the majority of people', 'most' is correct here, not 'more'. Double-check whether you mean 'additional amount' (more) or 'the majority' (most) before choosing.

front: This is far more better than the last one.
back: This is far better than the last one.
example: Since 'better' is already the correct comparative form, adding 'more' creates the same double comparative mistake covered earlier - 'far' alone is enough to add emphasis. Use intensifiers like 'far', 'much', or 'a lot' before an already-correct comparative, but never add 'more' as well.

front: She is the more talented singer in the group.
back: She is the most talented singer in the group.
example: Since this compares her to the WHOLE group (three or more people), the superlative 'most talented' is needed, not the comparative 'more talented' (which is only for comparing two things). Check whether you're comparing two things (comparative) or three or more (superlative) before choosing your form.

front: Between the two books, this is the best one.
back: Between the two books, this is the better one.
example: Since only TWO things are being compared here ('between the two'), the comparative form 'better' is correct, not the superlative 'best' (which is reserved for three or more things). Remember: comparative for two things, superlative for three or more - 'between' often signals exactly two.

front: He is the taller person in our whole class.
back: He is the tallest person in our whole class.
example: Since 'our whole class' includes many people (more than two), the superlative 'tallest' is needed, not the comparative 'taller'. Whenever you're comparing someone to a large group, use the superlative form, not comparative.

front: This cake tastes gooder than the last one.
back: This cake tastes better than the last one.
example: This repeats the irregular good/better/best mistake - 'gooder' is never correct; always use 'better' for comparative. This irregular pattern is worth practicing until it's completely automatic, since it's one of the most commonly used comparatives.

front: She runs more faster than anyone else.
back: She runs faster than anyone else.
example: 'Fast' is a short, one-syllable word, so it takes '-er' directly (faster), and adding 'more' creates a double comparative. Keep practicing this rule with short adjectives and adverbs: one syllable = '-er/-est' only.

front: This is the most fastest car in the race.
back: This is the fastest car in the race.
example: Just like 'faster', 'fastest' already carries the superlative meaning, so 'most' in front is unnecessary repetition. This follows the exact same rule as tallest, smartest, and other short-word superlatives.

front: My score was more high than his.
back: My score was higher than his.
example: 'High' is a short, one-syllable word, so it takes '-er' directly (higher), not 'more' in front. Continue reinforcing the short-word rule: one syllable adjectives get '-er/-est', not 'more/most'.

front: This is the most highest score ever recorded.
back: This is the highest score ever recorded.
example: 'Highest' already contains the superlative ending, making 'most' redundant when added in front. This is the same pattern seen with tallest, fastest, and smartest - all short adjectives forming their own superlative directly.

front: He is more strong than he looks.
back: He is stronger than he looks.
example: 'Strong' is a short, one-syllable word, so it takes '-er' directly (stronger), not 'more'. Keep applying the same consistent rule across all short adjectives you encounter.

front: This is the most strongest metal known.
back: This is the strongest metal known.
example: Just as before, 'strongest' already has the superlative ending built in, so 'most' is unnecessary. By now, this pattern should feel very familiar across many different short adjectives.

front: She is more clever than her classmates.
back: She is cleverer than her classmates. / She is more clever than her classmates (both acceptable).
example: 'Clever' is a two-syllable word that can actually take either form in modern English - 'cleverer' or 'more clever' are both considered acceptable, unlike stricter two-syllable words. This is one of a small group of two-syllable adjectives with flexible comparative forms, so both options work here.

front: This is more quiet than the library.
back: This is quieter than the library. / This is more quiet than the library (both acceptable).
example: Similar to 'clever', 'quiet' is another flexible two-syllable adjective where both 'quieter' and 'more quiet' are considered correct in modern usage. Recognize that a handful of two-syllable words allow both forms, unlike the stricter rule for most others.

front: He worked more hard than everyone else.
back: He worked harder than everyone else.
example: 'Hard' is a short, one-syllable adverb here, so it takes '-er' directly (harder), not 'more' in front. This same '-er/-est' rule applies to short adverbs, not just adjectives.

front: She arrived more early than expected.
back: She arrived earlier than expected.
example: 'Early' follows the two-syllable, y-ending pattern (like happy and easy), becoming 'earlier' directly, not 'more early'. Recognize this pattern even when the word functions as an adverb, not just as an adjective.

front: This is the more difficult exam of the year.
back: This is the most difficult exam of the year.
example: Since this compares to the WHOLE year (many exams, not just two), the superlative 'most difficult' is needed, not the comparative. Whenever a comparison involves 'of the year', 'in the class', or similar large groups, use the superlative form.

front: Of the two choices, this is the most practical.
back: Of the two choices, this is the more practical.
example: Since only two choices are being compared ('of the two'), the comparative 'more practical' is correct, not the superlative 'most practical'. Watch for phrases like 'of the two' or 'between the two', which always signal comparative, not superlative.

front: This is more unique than anything I've seen.
back: This is more unusual than anything I've seen. / This is unlike anything I've seen.
example: 'Unique' technically means 'one of a kind', so it can't logically be compared in degrees (something can't be 'more one-of-a-kind') - though this rule is often relaxed in casual speech. For precise, formal writing, avoid comparing 'unique' and use alternative words like 'unusual' or 'rare' instead.

front: This is the most perfect solution.
back: This is the ideal solution. / This is a perfect solution.
example: Just like 'unique', 'perfect' is technically an absolute quality (something is either perfect or it isn't), so adding 'most' doesn't logically work, though it's extremely common in casual speech. For careful writing, avoid intensifying 'perfect' with 'most' or 'more'.

front: This is more identical to the original than that one.
back: This is more similar to the original than that one. / This is identical to the original.
example: 'Identical' means 'exactly the same', which is also an absolute quality that can't logically have degrees - use 'similar' instead if you want to express varying degrees of closeness. Save 'identical' for describing an exact, complete match, without comparative modifiers.

front: He is the more experienced person in the company.
back: He is the most experienced person in the company.
example: Since 'the company' includes many employees (more than two), this needs the superlative 'most experienced', not the comparative. Keep checking group size whenever you use 'the' before a comparative or superlative form.

front: This laptop is more expensive than any laptop.
back: This laptop is more expensive than any other laptop.
