front: Amn't I invited to the party?
back: Aren't I invited to the party?
example: Although 'I am' would logically seem to need 'amn't' as its question form, that word isn't standard English - the accepted irregular form for 'I am not' in a question is 'aren't I', not 'amn't I' or 'ain't I'. This is a quirky exception worth memorizing on its own, since it doesn't follow the usual pattern.

front: How do you spell it that word?
back: How do you spell that word?
example: This sentence has both 'it' and 'that word' referring to the same thing, which is redundant - only one reference to the word is needed. Remove the extra pronoun 'it' since 'that word' already identifies what you're asking about.

back: What happened to you?
example: This sentence incorrectly adds 'did' into a question where 'what' is already the subject performing the action (happened) - no extra helping verb is needed. Remember the subject-question exception: when 'what' asks about the subject, skip do/does/did entirely.

back: Who are you?
example: Even though 'who' starts the question, the helping verb 'are' must still come before the subject 'you', following normal inversion rules. This mistake often happens because 'who' feels like it should behave differently, but it follows the same inversion pattern as other question words when it's not the subject.

front: Why do you not like it?
back: Why don't you like it?
example: While 'why do you not like it' is technically grammatical, the much more natural and common form contracts 'do not' into 'don't' in this question pattern. In everyday spoken and written English, always prefer the contracted form 'don't' in negative questions like this.

front: How is it possible that you knew?
back: How did you know? / How is it possible that you knew?
example: Both versions are actually acceptable here - the first is a simpler, more direct question, while the second is a more emphatic, formal version expressing surprise. Choose the simpler direct question for everyday speech, and save the more elaborate version for expressing genuine astonishment.

front: What do you think about it think?
back: What do you think about it?
example: This sentence mistakenly repeats the word 'think' at the end - the question only needs it once, right after 'do you'. Always check your questions for accidentally repeated words, especially in longer sentences.

front: Isn't it, that this is correct?
back: Isn't this correct? / This is correct, isn't it?
example: This sentence awkwardly combines a tag question format with unnecessary extra words - choose either a clean direct question ('Isn't this correct?') or a proper statement-plus-tag structure ('This is correct, isn't it?'). Avoid mixing partial structures together into one confusing sentence.

front: He said that he is tired.
back: He said that he was tired.
example: When the reporting verb ('said') is in the past tense, the tense inside the reported clause usually shifts back one step too - present simple ('is') becomes past simple ('was'). This shift is called 'backshifting', and it applies to almost all reported statements about the past.

front: She said that she is going to the market.
back: She said that she was going to the market.
example: Just like the previous example, present continuous ('is going') shifts back to past continuous ('was going') when reporting what someone said in the past. Keep applying this same backshift rule consistently across all present tenses.

front: He told me that he will help me.
back: He told me that he would help me.
example: The future tense marker 'will' shifts back to 'would' in reported speech, following the same backshifting pattern as other tenses. Remember this specific shift: will becomes would when reporting past speech.

front: She said that she can swim well.
back: She said that she could swim well.
example: The modal verb 'can' shifts back to 'could' in reported speech, just as other tenses shift back one step. Memorize this modal shift alongside will/would: can becomes could.

front: He said that he has finished the work.
back: He said that he had finished the work.
example: Present perfect ('has finished') shifts back to past perfect ('had finished') in reported speech. This follows the same backshifting logic - present perfect moves back to past perfect, just as present simple moves to past simple.

front: She said that she went there yesterday.
back: She said that she had gone there the day before.
example: This has two fixes: past simple ('went') often shifts back to past perfect ('had gone') in reported speech, and the specific time word 'yesterday' must change to 'the day before' since we're no longer speaking on that same day. Whenever reporting speech from a different day, adjust both the tense and any time expressions.

front: He asked me did I like the movie.
back: He asked me if I liked the movie.
example: Reported yes/no questions need 'if' or 'whether' added, and the word order returns to normal statement order (no more question inversion). Never use direct question word order (did I) inside reported speech - always convert to statement order with if/whether.

front: She asked me where do I live.
back: She asked me where I lived.
example: Just like yes/no questions, reported WH-questions (where, what, why) also drop the question word order and inversion, returning to normal statement order. Remove 'do/does/did' entirely and shift the tense onto the main verb.

front: He said, I will call you tomorrow, he said this yesterday.
back: He said he would call me the next day.
example: This example shows multiple needed shifts together: 'will' becomes 'would', 'you' becomes 'me' (since the reporter is the one being called), and 'tomorrow' becomes 'the next day' since we're not speaking on the same day as the original statement. Reported speech often requires several simultaneous shifts: tense, pronoun, and time word.

front: She told me that she is coming today.
back: She told me that she was coming that day.
example: This combines two shifts: 'is coming' becomes 'was coming' (backshift), and 'today' becomes 'that day' since the reporting happens at a different time than the original statement. Whenever reporting speech later, adjust time words that refer to 'now' into words that refer to 'then'.

front: He said that he will come here.
back: He said that he would come there.
example: Besides 'will' shifting to 'would', the place word 'here' must shift to 'there' since the reporter is typically not in the same location as the original speaker was referring to. Remember this place shift alongside the tense shift: here becomes there in reported speech.

front: She said she must leave immediately.
back: She said she had to leave immediately. / She said she must leave immediately (both acceptable).
example: 'Must' (meaning obligation) can shift to 'had to' in reported speech, though 'must' can also stay unchanged in many cases, especially for strong obligation or rules. This modal is a partial exception - it has some flexibility in reported speech, unlike 'will' and 'can', which always shift.

front: He asked, "What time is it?" and I said I don't know.
back: He asked what time it was, and I said I didn't know.
example: This sentence needs the reported question to be properly converted with statement word order, and the response also needs the standard backshift from 'don't' to 'didn't'. Apply the backshift rule to every reported clause in a sentence, not just the first one.

front: She said that this is her book.
back: She said that was her book. / She said that this was her book.
example: Beyond the tense shift (is to was), the demonstrative pronoun 'this' often shifts to 'that' in reported speech when referring to something not physically present at the time of reporting. Consider shifting 'this/these' to 'that/those' when reporting speech at a later time or in a different place.

front: He said he would come, but he said it yesterday and now it's tomorrow.
back: He said he would come.
example: This sentence is unnecessarily complicated - reported speech doesn't need you to explain the exact timing of the original statement separately; the backshifted tense ('would') already implies this happened in the past relative to now. Trust that the backshifted verb tense already carries the necessary time relationship, without over-explaining.

front: She asked me can I help her.
back: She asked me if I could help her.
example: This combines two fixes: add 'if' since this is a yes/no question, and shift 'can' back to 'could' following the standard modal backshift rule. Both fixes (adding if/whether AND backshifting the modal) are usually needed together in reported yes/no questions.

front: He told me that he doesn't like coffee.
back: He told me that he didn't like coffee.
example: Present simple negative ('doesn't like') shifts back to past simple negative ('didn't like') in reported speech, following the same backshift rule as positive sentences. Negative sentences follow exactly the same backshifting pattern as positive ones - don't forget to shift these too.

front: She said that she isn't coming to the party.
back: She said that she wasn't coming to the party.
example: Just like the previous example, present continuous negative ('isn't coming') shifts to past continuous negative ('wasn't coming'). Keep applying the backshift rule consistently to every verb form you encounter, whether positive or negative.

front: He said he has never been to Paris.
back: He said he had never been to Paris.
example: Present perfect ('has been') shifts to past perfect ('had been') in reported speech, even with 'never' inserted in the middle. The presence of 'never' or other adverbs doesn't change the backshift rule - it still applies to the main verb tense.

front: She told me she is going to study medicine.
back: She told me she was going to study medicine.
example: The 'going to' future form shifts back to its past tense version ('was going to') in reported speech, just like other present tenses. Remember that 'going to' future follows the same backshift pattern as present continuous, since it uses the same verb structure.

front: He asked where I am going.
back: He asked where I was going.
example: Even in this embedded question (already in normal statement order), the tense still needs to backshift from present ('am going') to past ('was going'). Don't forget the tense shift just because the question word order was already correct - both fixes are usually needed together.

front: She said, "I have already eaten," so she said she has already eaten.
back: She said that she had already eaten.
example: This sentence incorrectly keeps the present perfect ('has eaten') instead of shifting to past perfect ('had eaten'), which is required since the original statement itself was already in present perfect. When the original quote is in present perfect, the reported version shifts to past perfect.

front: He told me I should to call him back.
back: He told me I should call him back.
example: This is a separate mistake unrelated to backshifting - 'should' is a modal verb, and modal verbs are never followed by 'to' before the base verb. Remove 'to' after any modal verb (should, can, will, must), whether in reported speech or a direct statement.

front: She asked if I was here yesterday.
back: She asked if I had been there the day before.
example: This needs several shifts together: 'was' becomes 'had been' (past shifts to past perfect when reporting an even earlier past event), 'here' becomes 'there', and 'yesterday' becomes 'the day before'. Complex reported speech often requires multiple simultaneous shifts - practice identifying all of them in one sentence.

front: He said that he would came tomorrow.
back: He said that he would come the next day.
example: This has two issues: after 'would', the verb must stay in base form ('come'), not past tense ('came'), and 'tomorrow' should shift to 'the next day'. Always check the verb form after modals like would/could/should - they need the base verb, never a past tense form.

front: She told me that she needs my help.
back: She told me that she needed my help.
example: Present simple ('needs') shifts back to past simple ('needed') in reported speech, following the standard backshift rule. This is a simple, regular example of the core backshift rule applying to an ordinary present simple verb.

front: He asked me what I am doing here.
back: He asked me what I was doing there.
example: This needs two shifts: the tense backshifts from present continuous ('am doing') to past continuous ('was doing'), and the place word shifts from 'here' to 'there'. Combine the tense shift and place shift together whenever both apply in the same sentence.

front: She said that tomorrow she will visit her grandmother.
back: She said that the next day she would visit her grandmother.
example: This needs both the time word shift ('tomorrow' to 'the next day') and the modal shift ('will' to 'would'), applied together in the same sentence. Whenever a sentence has both a time word and a future tense marker, remember to shift both.

front: He told me he can't come today.
back: He told me he couldn't come that day.
example: This needs both the modal shift ('can't' to 'couldn't') and the time word shift ('today' to 'that day'), combined in one sentence. Practice looking for multiple elements needing change within a single reported sentence - modals, time words, and place words often appear together.

front: She asked whether I have finished my work.
back: She asked whether I had finished my work.
example: Even though 'whether' correctly introduces this reported yes/no question, the tense still needs to shift from present perfect ('have finished') to past perfect ('had finished'). Using 'whether' or 'if' correctly doesn't exempt you from also applying the tense backshift.

front: He said he is having lunch right now.
back: He said he was having lunch at that time.
example: This needs both the tense shift (present continuous to past continuous) and the time phrase shift ('right now' to 'at that time'), since 'right now' only makes sense from the original speaker's perspective at the moment of speaking. Time phrases describing the present moment ('now', 'right now', 'currently') typically shift to phrases describing a past moment when reporting later.

front: She told me that her sister lives here.
back: She told me that her sister lived there.
example: Present simple ('lives') shifts to past simple ('lived'), and 'here' shifts to 'there' since the reporter is describing a location relative to the original speaker's perspective. Whenever a place word appears in reported speech, consider whether it needs to shift along with the tense.

front: He asked if I will be free next week.
back: He asked if I would be free the following week.
example: This needs both the modal shift ('will' to 'would') and the time phrase shift ('next week' to 'the following week'). Always check for both a tense/modal shift and a time-word shift together whenever future time references appear in reported speech.

front: She said, "I bought this yesterday," so she said she bought this yesterday.
back: She said that she had bought that the day before.
example: This example needs multiple shifts together: past simple ('bought') often shifts to past perfect ('had bought'), 'this' shifts to 'that', and 'yesterday' shifts to 'the day before'. When reporting speech that already contains time and demonstrative words, expect several simultaneous shifts.

front: He told me he doesn't want to go there anymore.
back: He told me he didn't want to go there anymore.
example: Present simple negative ('doesn't want') shifts back to past simple negative ('didn't want'), following the standard rule even with the word 'anymore' included. Additional words like 'anymore' or 'still' don't change the basic backshift rule for the main verb.

front: She asked me if I am ready to leave.
back: She asked me if I was ready to leave.
example: Even with 'if' correctly used for this yes/no question, the verb 'am' still needs to shift back to 'was' in reported speech. Using the correct question-reporting word ('if') doesn't replace the need for tense backshifting.

front: He said that he was working on it and he'll finish soon.
back: He said that he was working on it and that he would finish soon.
example: The second half of this sentence still needs the future shift from 'will' to 'would', even though the first half was already correctly shifted. When a sentence has multiple clauses being reported, check that EACH one has the necessary tense shifts, not just the first.

front: She told me that she is thinking about moving abroad.
back: She told me that she was thinking about moving abroad.
example: Present continuous ('is thinking') shifts to past continuous ('was thinking'), following the standard backshift pattern once more. This is a straightforward, regular application of the core backshift rule for present continuous tense.

front: He asked me what time does the store close.
back: He asked me what time the store closed.
example: This needs two fixes: remove 'does' since this is now an embedded/reported question with normal word order, and shift the tense onto the main verb ('closed') directly. Combine the word-order fix (statement order, no do/does/did) with the tense backshift for a fully correct reported question.

front: She said she has been feeling unwell lately.
back: She said she had been feeling unwell lately.
example: Present perfect continuous ('has been feeling') shifts to past perfect continuous ('had been feeling') in reported speech. This shows the backshift rule extending to the more complex continuous perfect tenses as well, not just simple tenses.

front: He told me he will always remember this day.
back: He told me he would always remember that day.
example: This needs both the modal shift ('will' to 'would') and the demonstrative shift ('this day' to 'that day'), applied together. Whenever 'this' appears alongside a future tense marker, expect to shift both elements when reporting.

front: She asked if I can meet her tomorrow.
back: She asked if I could meet her the next day.
example: This combines the modal shift ('can' to 'could') with the time word shift ('tomorrow' to 'the next day'), both required together. This exact combination - modal plus time word - appears very frequently in reported speech exercises.

front: He said that he is planning to visit next month.
back: He said that he was planning to visit the following month.
example: This needs both the tense shift (present continuous to past continuous) and the time phrase shift ('next month' to 'the following month'). Keep practicing this combination until identifying both necessary shifts becomes automatic.

front: She told me she doesn't know the answer.
back: She told me she didn't know the answer.
example: This is a simple, direct application of the core backshift rule: present simple negative shifts to past simple negative. Practicing simple, clear examples like this one helps reinforce the fundamental rule before tackling more complex combined shifts.

front: He asked whether I was coming to the meeting today.
back: He asked whether I was coming to the meeting that day.
example: Even though the tense ('was coming') is already correctly in past continuous, the time word 'today' still needs to shift to 'that day' since we're reporting this at a different time. Don't forget to check time words even when the tense itself is already correctly shifted.

front: She said that she can't believe the news.
back: She said that she couldn't believe the news.
example: This is a direct, simple application of the modal backshift rule: can't shifts to couldn't. This example reinforces the modal-shift rule in its most straightforward form.

front: He told me he is not sure about the plan.
back: He told me he was not sure about the plan.
example: Present simple with 'be' ('is not sure') shifts to past simple ('was not sure'), following the same rule as all other present tense forms. This shows the backshift rule applying even to simple descriptive statements using 'be', not just action verbs.

front: She asked if I have any questions.
back: She asked if I had any questions.
example: Present simple ('have') shifts to past simple ('had') in this reported yes/no question, combining the 'if' structure with the standard tense backshift. This is one of the most common reported speech patterns in everyday conversation, especially in meetings and interviews.

front: He said that he was here now.
back: He said that he was there then.
example: Both 'here' and 'now' need to shift to 'there' and 'then' respectively, since the reporter isn't speaking from the same place or moment as the original speaker. Whenever both a place word and a time word appear together in the original quote, shift both consistently.

front: She told me that this was the best day of her life.
back: She told me that that was the best day of her life.
example: The demonstrative 'this' shifts to 'that' in reported speech when referring to something no longer immediate or present. Practice shifting demonstratives (this/these to that/those) alongside tense shifts whenever they appear in the original statement.

front: I went to the store I bought some milk.
back: I went to the store, and I bought some milk.
example: These are two complete sentences (each has its own subject and verb) joined with no punctuation at all, which is called a run-on sentence. Fix it by adding a comma plus a joining word (and, but, so), or by using a period or semicolon to separate them into two sentences.

front: She was tired, she went to bed early.
back: She was tired, so she went to bed early.
example: This is a comma splice - two complete sentences joined by only a comma, with no connecting word. A comma alone isn't strong enough to join two full sentences; add a joining word like 'so', 'and', or 'but' after the comma, or replace the comma with a period or semicolon.

front: It was raining, we stayed inside.
back: It was raining, so we stayed inside.
example: Just like the previous example, two complete ideas are joined with only a comma - this needs a connecting word to show the relationship between them (here, cause and effect). Adding 'so' shows that the rain caused them to stay inside.

front: I like coffee, she likes tea.
back: I like coffee, but she likes tea.
example: This comma splice needs a contrasting word like 'but' to show the two people have different preferences. Whenever two complete sentences show a contrast, join them with a comma plus 'but', not a comma alone.

front: He studied hard, he still failed the exam.
back: He studied hard, but he still failed the exam.
example: Since the second idea contrasts with the first (studying hard usually leads to passing, but here it didn't), 'but' is the correct connector. Always check whether your two ideas agree or contrast to choose between 'and' and 'but'.

front: The movie was long it was very boring.
back: The movie was long, and it was very boring.
example: Two complete sentences here are joined with no punctuation at all - add a comma and 'and' since both ideas support each other (long AND boring). Remember: a comma alone can never join two full sentences; it always needs a joining word with it (unless using a semicolon).

front: I finished my homework, I watched TV.
back: I finished my homework, and then I watched TV. / I finished my homework; then I watched TV.
example: This comma splice can be fixed by adding 'and then' to show the sequence of events, or by using a semicolon to join the two related sentences. Semicolons work well when two sentences are closely related in meaning but don't need a specific joining word.

front: She is smart, she works very hard.
back: She is smart, and she works very hard.
example: Both ideas support each other positively here, so 'and' is the correct connector after the comma. Whenever both parts of a sentence add similar or supporting information, 'and' is usually the right choice.

front: I wanted to go, I didn't have time.
back: I wanted to go, but I didn't have time.
example: Since the second part contrasts with the first (wanting to go, but not having time), 'but' is needed to correctly join these two complete sentences. This is a very common pattern in everyday speech, so practicing the correct comma + but structure is valuable.

front: The weather was cold, we wore jackets.
back: The weather was cold, so we wore jackets.
example: The second sentence shows the RESULT of the first (cold weather caused jacket-wearing), so 'so' is the correct connecting word. Whenever one idea causes or leads to the next, use a comma plus 'so'.

front: I have a lot of work, I can't come to the party.
back: I have a lot of work, so I can't come to the party.
example: Again, the second part is the RESULT of the first (a lot of work leads to not being able to attend), so 'so' fits correctly. Practice identifying cause-and-effect relationships to know when 'so' is the right connector.

front: He is a good friend, he always helps me.
back: He is a good friend, and he always helps me.
example: Both statements support the same positive idea about this person, so 'and' correctly joins them. When both parts of your sentence point in the same direction (both positive or both negative), 'and' usually works well.

front: I called her twice, she didn't answer.
back: I called her twice, but she didn't answer.
example: The second part contrasts with the expected outcome of the first (calling twice, but still no answer), so 'but' is correct. This shows an unexpected or disappointing result, which 'but' signals clearly.

front: The traffic was heavy, I arrived late.
back: The traffic was heavy, so I arrived late.
example: Since arriving late is the RESULT of heavy traffic, 'so' is the correct connecting word here. Cause-and-effect relationships almost always need 'so' to link them properly with a comma.

front: She loves music, she plays the piano every day.
back: She loves music, and she plays the piano every day.
example: Both statements support the same idea (her passion for music), making 'and' the correct choice. Whenever your two sentences build on the same overall point, 'and' ties them together smoothly.

front: I am tired, I need to rest.
back: I am tired, so I need to rest.
example: The need to rest is a direct RESULT of being tired, so 'so' correctly shows this cause-and-effect relationship. This is one of the most natural, common sentence patterns in daily English, worth practicing directly.

back: He apologized, and she forgave him. / He apologized, so she forgave him.
example: Both 'and' (simply listing two related events) and 'so' (showing his apology caused her forgiveness) work here, depending on the exact relationship you want to express. Choose 'so' if you want to emphasize cause-and-effect, or 'and' for a simple sequence.

front: I don't like spicy food, my brother loves it.
back: I don't like spicy food, but my brother loves it.
example: Since this shows a contrast between two people's preferences, 'but' is the correct connecting word. Whenever you compare two different opinions or preferences, 'but' signals that contrast clearly.

front: We left early, we wanted to avoid traffic.
back: We left early because we wanted to avoid traffic. / We left early to avoid traffic.
example: Here, the second part explains the REASON for the first action, so 'because' (not just a comma) is the clearest connector. Whenever the second part explains 'why' the first part happened, use 'because' to join them properly.

front: It started to rain, we went inside quickly.
back: It started to rain, so we went inside quickly.
example: Going inside is the RESULT of the rain starting, making 'so' the correct choice to link these two events. This is another clear cause-and-effect pattern, similar to several examples already covered.

front: I read the instructions carefully, I still made a mistake.
back: I read the instructions carefully, but I still made a mistake.
example: The unexpected outcome here (still making a mistake despite careful reading) needs 'but' to show the contrast. Whenever something happens despite an effort, use 'but' to connect the two ideas.

front: She practiced every day, she became an expert.
back: She practiced every day, and she became an expert. / She practiced every day, so she became an expert.
example: Depending on emphasis, both 'and' (simple sequence) and 'so' (result of practice) can work correctly here. Consider what relationship you want to highlight - simple listing, or clear cause and effect.

front: I saved money for a year, I bought a new phone.
back: I saved money for a year, so I bought a new phone.
example: Buying the phone is the direct RESULT of saving money, so 'so' correctly connects these two complete sentences. This pattern - saving leading to buying - is a classic cause-and-effect relationship.

front: The book was interesting, I finished it in one day.
back: The book was interesting, so I finished it in one day.
example: Finishing the book quickly is a RESULT of it being interesting, making 'so' the correct connector. Whenever an outcome directly follows from a quality or condition, use 'so'.

front: He is very talented, he never practices.
back: He is very talented, but he never practices.
example: This shows a surprising contrast (talent without practice), so 'but' correctly links these ideas. Contrast words like 'but' are essential whenever two facts seem to work against each other.

front: I wanted to help, I didn't know how.
back: I wanted to help, but I didn't know how.
example: The second part contrasts with the intention expressed in the first (wanting to help, but lacking knowledge on how), so 'but' fits correctly. This pattern of good intention followed by a limitation is very common in daily conversation.

front: She sent the email, she forgot the attachment.
back: She sent the email, but she forgot the attachment.
example: Forgetting the attachment is an unfortunate contrast to the action of sending the email, so 'but' correctly shows this. Whenever a mistake or oversight follows an action, 'but' usually links them well.

front: I studied all night, I still felt unprepared.
back: I studied all night, but I still felt unprepared.
example: The unexpected outcome (still feeling unprepared despite studying) requires 'but' to show the contrast clearly. This is a very relatable, common sentence pattern about effort not matching the expected outcome.

front: The team worked together, they won the championship.
back: The team worked together, and they won the championship. / The team worked together, so they won the championship.
example: Depending on your intended emphasis, either 'and' (listing the events) or 'so' (showing teamwork caused the win) works well here. Consider whether you want to simply connect two facts, or highlight one causing the other.

front: I missed the bus, I had to walk to work.
back: I missed the bus, so I had to walk to work.
example: Walking to work is the direct RESULT of missing the bus, making 'so' the correct connector. This cause-and-effect pattern appears very frequently in everyday storytelling.

front: She is allergic to nuts, she avoids all nut products.
back: She is allergic to nuts, so she avoids all nut products.
example: Avoiding nut products is a direct RESULT of the allergy, so 'so' correctly connects these two complete sentences. Whenever a behavior is explained by a preceding fact or condition, 'so' usually fits.

front: I forgot my umbrella, I got completely soaked.
back: I forgot my umbrella, so I got completely soaked.
example: Getting soaked is the direct RESULT of forgetting the umbrella, making 'so' the natural connector. This is another classic cause-and-effect storytelling pattern.

front: He speaks three languages, he learned them all as a child.
back: He speaks three languages, and he learned them all as a child. / He speaks three languages because he learned them all as a child.
example: You can either simply connect these facts with 'and', or show the explanation more directly with 'because'. Choose 'because' if you want to emphasize WHY he speaks three languages.

front: The store was closed, we went to a different one.
back: The store was closed, so we went to a different one.
example: Going to a different store is the RESULT of the first store being closed, so 'so' is correct here. This is a very typical everyday situation showing clear cause and effect.

front: I didn't have my phone, I couldn't call for help.
back: I didn't have my phone, so I couldn't call for help.
example: Not being able to call for help is the direct RESULT of not having a phone, making 'so' the correct connecting word. This pattern of 'lacking something leading to an inability' is very common.

front: She works two jobs, she is always exhausted.
back: She works two jobs, so she is always exhausted.
example: Being exhausted is the RESULT of working two jobs, so 'so' correctly links these two complete sentences. Whenever a state of being (tired, happy, stressed) follows logically from a situation, 'so' usually connects them well.

front: I planted the seeds in spring, they bloomed by summer.
back: I planted the seeds in spring, and they bloomed by summer.
example: These two events happen in sequence and support the same overall story, so 'and' works well to connect them. When events simply happen one after another as part of a natural sequence, 'and' is often the best, simplest choice.

front: I lost my keys, I had to call a locksmith.
back: I lost my keys, so I had to call a locksmith.
example: Calling a locksmith is the direct RESULT of losing the keys, so 'so' correctly connects these two ideas. This is yet another clear example of the cause-and-effect pattern that appears throughout everyday English.

front: The recipe looked easy, it turned out to be quite difficult.
back: The recipe looked easy, but it turned out to be quite difficult.
example: This shows an unexpected contrast between appearance (looked easy) and reality (was difficult), so 'but' is the correct connector. Whenever expectations don't match reality, 'but' signals that surprising contrast.

front: I exercise every morning, I feel more energetic.
back: I exercise every morning, so I feel more energetic. / I exercise every morning, and I feel more energetic.
example: You can use 'so' to emphasize that exercise causes the energy, or 'and' to simply list both facts together. Think about whether you want to stress the cause-and-effect relationship, or just connect two related facts.

front: He locked the door, he left for work.
back: He locked the door, and he left for work. / He locked the door before he left for work.
example: These are sequential actions, so 'and' works to simply connect them, or you can use 'before' to show the specific order of events more precisely. Consider whether the order of actions matters enough to specify with 'before' or 'after'.

front: I turned off the lights, I went to sleep.
back: I turned off the lights, and then I went to sleep.
example: Adding 'and then' clarifies the sequence of these two actions, fixing the comma splice while showing the order clearly. Whenever actions happen one after another, 'and then' is a natural, clear way to connect them.

front: She checked her email, she replied to three messages.
back: She checked her email, and she replied to three messages.
example: These two actions are simply sequential parts of the same activity, so 'and' correctly joins them. When describing a series of related steps someone took, 'and' is usually the simplest, most natural connector.

front: The company grew quickly, they hired many new employees.
back: The company grew quickly, so they hired many new employees.
example: Hiring new employees is the RESULT of quick growth, so 'so' is the correct word to connect these two complete sentences. This is a common business-related cause-and-effect pattern.

front: I read the review, I decided not to watch the movie.
back: I read the review, so I decided not to watch the movie.
example: The decision not to watch is a direct RESULT of reading the review, making 'so' the correct connector. This shows how a review (the cause) led to a decision (the effect).

front: The internet was down, we couldn't finish the meeting online.
back: The internet was down, so we couldn't finish the meeting online.
example: The inability to finish the meeting is the RESULT of the internet outage, so 'so' correctly links these ideas. This is another everyday technology-related cause-and-effect example.

front: He trained for months, he finally ran the marathon.
back: He trained for months, and he finally ran the marathon.
example: These two facts build on the same overall achievement story, so 'and' works well to connect them here. When both parts of a sentence build toward the same accomplishment, 'and' ties them together naturally.

front: I checked the weather forecast, I decided to bring an umbrella.
back: I checked the weather forecast, so I decided to bring an umbrella.
example: Deciding to bring an umbrella is the direct RESULT of checking the forecast, making 'so' the correct connector. This everyday decision-making pattern appears constantly in daily conversation.

front: The flight was delayed, we missed our connecting flight.
back: The flight was delayed, so we missed our connecting flight.
example: Missing the connection is the direct RESULT of the delay, so 'so' correctly joins these two complete sentences. This travel-related example shows a very relatable everyday frustration.

front: I proofread my essay twice, I still found an error.
back: I proofread my essay twice, but I still found an error.
example: This shows a surprising contrast (proofreading carefully, yet still finding a mistake), so 'but' correctly connects these ideas. Whenever careful effort doesn't fully prevent a problem, 'but' captures that disappointing contrast.

back: I went to bed because I was tired.
example: This fragment starts with 'because', which makes it a dependent clause - it explains a reason but can't stand alone without a main sentence attached. Whenever a sentence begins with 'because', 'although', 'since', or 'when', make sure it's connected to a complete main clause.

back: She was running through the park.
example: This fragment has no clear subject or complete verb - 'running' needs a helping verb (was/is/were) and a subject to become a full sentence. Always check that your sentence has both someone/something doing the action AND a complete verb form.

back: The book on the table is mine.
example: This fragment only has a subject (the book) with a description (on the table), but no verb telling us what happens - it needs a verb to complete the thought. Ask yourself: what is happening, or what is being said about the subject? Add that missing verb.

back: Although she studied hard, she didn't pass the exam.
example: Just like 'because', 'although' introduces a dependent clause that needs a main clause to complete the thought - it can't stand alone as a full sentence. Whenever you start with 'although' or 'even though', continue the sentence with what happened despite that condition.

front: When I arrived at the station.
back: When I arrived at the station, the train had already left.
example: This fragment describes a time ('when I arrived') but doesn't tell us what happened at that time - it needs a main clause attached. Time clauses starting with 'when', 'while', or 'as soon as' always need to be connected to a complete sentence.

back: She is a very talented singer.
example: This fragment describes something but has no subject or verb showing who or what it's describing. Add a subject (she, he, the artist) and a verb (is, was) to complete the sentence.

back: He is working late every night.
example: Similar to the earlier example, this fragment has an action word ('working') but lacks a subject and helping verb to make it complete. Add 'he is', 'she was', or another subject plus helping verb before the action.

front: Which is why I left early.
back: I felt sick, which is why I left early.
example: This fragment begins with 'which', referring back to an idea that isn't stated - it needs the first part of the sentence to make sense. Whenever you start a sentence with 'which', check that the thing 'which' refers to is already mentioned.

back: He was standing alone in the rain.
example: This fragment describes an action ('standing') without telling us who is doing it or providing a complete verb form. Add a subject and helping verb: 'he was standing', 'she is standing', to complete the thought.

back: Even though it was late, we decided to continue working.
example: Just like 'although', the phrase 'even though' introduces a condition that needs a main clause to complete the meaning. This dependent clause pattern is one of the most common causes of sentence fragments, so watch for these starting words carefully.

back: Since I have no money, I can't go shopping today.
example: 'Since' here introduces a reason, making this a dependent clause that needs a main clause attached to form a complete sentence. Whenever 'since' means 'because' at the start of a sentence, remember it needs a full second part.

front: The man wearing a blue shirt.
back: The man wearing a blue shirt is my uncle.
example: This fragment describes a person with detail (wearing a blue shirt) but never tells us what he does or what is said about him - a verb is missing. Complete the thought by adding a verb: is, was, walked, spoke, etc.

front: If you have time this weekend.
back: If you have time this weekend, let's meet for coffee.
example: 'If' introduces a condition, and conditions always need a result attached to become complete - this fragment is missing that result. Whenever you start with 'if', make sure to finish the thought with what would happen under that condition.

back: After finishing my homework, I watched a movie.
example: 'After' introduces a time relationship, but this fragment doesn't say what happened after - it needs a main clause. Time words like 'after' and 'before' always need to connect to a complete action or event.

back: Before you leave the house, please lock the door.
example: Just like 'after', 'before' sets up a time condition that needs to be finished with the actual instruction or event. This fragment leaves the reader wondering: before you leave, what should happen?

front: A beautiful sunset over the ocean.
back: We watched a beautiful sunset over the ocean.
example: This fragment is just a descriptive phrase with no subject performing an action or verb showing what happened. Add who is experiencing or doing something with this sunset, plus a verb.

back: While waiting for the bus, I read a book.
example: 'While' introduces a time relationship for something happening simultaneously, and this fragment needs the second event attached. Complete it by adding what you were doing during that waiting time.

back: Unless you apologize to her, she won't forgive you.
example: 'Unless' sets up a condition (similar to 'if not'), and this fragment is missing the result of that condition. Always finish an 'unless' clause with what will or won't happen.

back: He was trying his best to succeed.
example: This fragment has an action ('trying') but no clear subject and helping verb attached to make it a complete sentence. Add a subject and 'was/is' before the -ing verb to complete it.

back: Because of the heavy rain, the match was postponed.
example: 'Because of' introduces a reason but this phrase alone doesn't tell us the RESULT of that reason - a main clause needs to follow. This is different from 'because' alone (a full clause) - 'because of' is followed by a noun phrase, then needs its own separate main clause.

back: She won the competition, which made everyone very happy.
example: Starting a sentence with 'which' referring to something not yet mentioned leaves the reader confused about what 'which' refers to. Always attach this kind of clause to the sentence describing the event it refers to.

front: The girl who always arrives late.
back: The girl who always arrives late missed the meeting today.
example: This fragment describes a person in detail (who always arrives late) but never says what she actually did - the main verb for the sentence is missing. Complete the thought with what this person did or does.

front: In order to save money for college.
back: In order to save money for college, she took a part-time job.
example: 'In order to' introduces a purpose, but the fragment doesn't say what action was taken for that purpose. Complete it by stating the action someone took to achieve that goal.

front: As soon as the bell rings.
back: As soon as the bell rings, the students will leave the classroom.
example: 'As soon as' sets up a time condition, and this fragment is missing what happens at that moment. Always follow 'as soon as' with the resulting action.

back: Despite trying really hard, he didn't win the race.
example: 'Despite' introduces a contrast, and this fragment doesn't complete that contrast with what actually happened. Whenever you start with 'despite' or 'in spite of', finish with the surprising or contrasting outcome.

front: So that everyone could hear clearly.
back: She spoke loudly so that everyone could hear clearly.
example: 'So that' introduces a purpose, but this fragment doesn't tell us the action taken for that purpose. Complete the sentence with what was done to achieve this goal.

front: Considering how difficult the test was.
back: Considering how difficult the test was, she did very well.
example: 'Considering' introduces a factor to think about, but this fragment doesn't complete the thought about what happened given that factor. Add the main point or outcome you want to relate to this consideration.

front: The team that won the championship last year.
back: The team that won the championship last year is playing again tonight.
example: This fragment describes a team in detail but never says what the team is doing now - the main verb is missing. Add a verb describing the team's current action or state.

front: Provided that you finish on time.
back: Provided that you finish on time, you can leave early.
example: 'Provided that' sets up a condition (similar to 'if'), and needs a result attached to complete the sentence. Always follow conditional phrases like this with what will happen if the condition is met.

back: Not knowing what to say, she stayed silent.
example: This fragment describes a state ('not knowing') but doesn't tell us what the person did as a result. Complete it by adding what action followed from that uncertainty.

back: Given the current circumstances, we need to postpone the event.
example: 'Given' here introduces a condition or context, but this fragment needs a main clause to state the actual decision or outcome. Complete the thought with what should be done considering this context.

back: Whatever you decide to do, I will support you.
example: 'Whatever' introduces an open condition, and this fragment is missing the promise or statement connected to it. Complete it with what will happen regardless of the choice made.

back: The reason for her absence was unclear.
example: This fragment names a topic (the reason) but doesn't say anything about it - a verb is needed to complete the thought. Add a verb like 'was', 'remains', or 'is' to finish the sentence.

back: To become a successful entrepreneur, you need patience and hard work.
example: This fragment states a goal ('to become...') but doesn't say what's needed to achieve it - it's an incomplete infinitive phrase needing a main clause. Whenever a sentence starts with 'to + verb' describing a goal, complete it with what's required or what happens.

back: Regardless of the outcome, we tried our best.
example: 'Regardless of' introduces a condition being dismissed as unimportant, and this fragment needs the main point attached. Complete the thought with what remains true no matter the result.

back: Having finished all my chores, I relaxed on the couch.
example: This fragment describes a completed action ('having finished') but doesn't say what happened next - it needs a main clause. Whenever you start with 'having + past participle', follow it with what you did afterward.

front: Which is something I've always wanted.
back: I finally got the job, which is something I've always wanted.
example: Starting with 'which' here refers to something not yet mentioned in this fragment, leaving the reader confused. Always attach this clause to the sentence describing what 'which' refers back to.

front: As long as you try your best.
back: As long as you try your best, that's all that matters.
example: 'As long as' introduces a condition, and this fragment needs the result or consequence attached to complete the sentence. Complete conditional phrases like this with what follows from meeting the condition.

front: Whether we like it or not.
back: Whether we like it or not, we have to follow the rules.
example: 'Whether...or not' introduces two possibilities, but this fragment doesn't state what remains true regardless of the choice. Complete it with the outcome that applies either way.

back: Until the situation improves, we will continue working from home.
example: 'Until' sets up a time limit, and this fragment needs the ongoing action attached to complete the thought. Whenever you use 'until' to mark an endpoint, state what continues happening before that point.

front: Assuming everything goes according to plan.
back: Assuming everything goes according to plan, we should finish by Friday.
example: 'Assuming' introduces a hypothetical condition, and this fragment needs the expected outcome attached. Complete conditional phrases starting with 'assuming' by adding what you expect will happen.

front: For the sake of the team's success.
back: For the sake of the team's success, everyone worked overtime.
example: 'For the sake of' introduces a purpose or motivation, but this fragment doesn't say what action was taken for that purpose. Complete it with the action people took motivated by this goal.

front: The people who live next door.
back: The people who live next door are very friendly.
example: This fragment describes a group of people in detail but never says anything about them - the main verb is missing. Add a verb describing something about these people.

back: According to the latest report, sales have increased by 20%.
example: 'According to' introduces a source of information, and this fragment needs the actual information attached to complete the sentence. Whenever you cite a source with 'according to', follow it with what that source says.

front: In spite of all the challenges we faced.
back: In spite of all the challenges we faced, we completed the project.
example: 'In spite of' introduces a contrast, similar to 'despite', and this fragment needs the surprising positive outcome attached. Complete contrast phrases like this with what happened anyway, despite the difficulty mentioned.

back: As far as I know, the meeting is still scheduled for tomorrow.
example: 'As far as I know' introduces a qualified statement of knowledge, and this fragment needs the actual information attached. Complete it with what you believe to be true, based on your limited knowledge.

front: Once you understand the basic rules.
back: Once you understand the basic rules, the game becomes much easier.
example: 'Once' introduces a time condition (meaning 'after'), and this fragment needs the resulting situation attached. Complete time clauses starting with 'once' by adding what happens after that point.

back: Whoever finishes first will receive a prize.
example: 'Whoever' introduces an open subject referring to an unknown person, and this fragment needs a verb telling us what happens to that person. Complete it with the action or result that applies to whoever fits the description.

front: Now that the exams are over.
back: Now that the exams are over, we can finally relax.
example: 'Now that' introduces a reason related to timing, and this fragment needs the resulting action or feeling attached. Complete time-related reason clauses like this with what can now happen.

back: Seeing how tired she looked, I offered to help.
example: This fragment describes an observation ('seeing how tired she looked') but doesn't say what action followed from that observation. Complete it with what you did in response to what you noticed.

front: She go to school every day.
back: She goes to school every day.
example: In the present simple tense, verbs must end in '-s' or '-es' whenever the subject is he, she, or it. 'She go' skips this rule. To fix it in future, ask yourself: can I replace the subject with he/she/it? If yes, the verb needs an 's' at the end.

back: He doesn't like coffee.
example: 'Don't' is short for 'do not', which is only used with I/you/we/they. With he/she/it, you must use 'doesn't' (does not). A simple way to remember this: he/she/it always pairs with 'doesn't', never 'don't'.

back: The dogs bark loudly.
example: 'Dogs' is plural, so the verb should not have an 's' - only singular subjects (he/she/it) take an '-s' verb ending. As a rule, plural subjects always take the plain form of the verb, with no extra letters added.

back: Everyone is happy today.
example: Words like everyone, everybody, someone, and anybody sound like they mean 'many people', but grammatically they are always treated as one single person, so they take a singular verb. Whenever you see 'every-' or 'some-' combined with 'one/body', use is/was/has, never are/were/have.

back: The team is winning.
example: Words describing a group - team, family, class, committee - are treated as one single unit when they act together, so they take a singular verb. Only use the plural form if you are talking about the individual members doing different things separately.

front: Neither of the boys were late.
back: Neither of the boys was late.
example: 'Neither' means 'not one of the two', so even though it's followed by a plural noun (boys), the true meaning is singular. Whenever a sentence starts with 'neither of' or 'either of', use a singular verb.

front: The list of items are on the table.
back: The list of items is on the table.
example: The real subject here is 'list', not 'items' - the phrase 'of items' is just extra description. A helpful trick: cover up any 'of + noun' phrase with your hand, and match the verb to whatever word is left.

front: Each of the students have a book.
back: Each of the students has a book.
example: 'Each' always refers to one person at a time, even when it's followed by a plural noun like 'students'. Whenever a sentence begins with 'each of', treat it as singular and use has/is/was.

back: My family is very supportive.
example: When you talk about your family as one unit that supports you together, use the singular verb. (In British English, 'family are' is sometimes used when talking about members acting individually, but for learners the safest and most common choice is singular.)

front: Ten dollars are too much for this.
back: Ten dollars is too much for this.
example: When an amount of money, time, weight, or distance is being discussed as a single total quantity, English treats it as one idea and uses a singular verb, even though the number looks plural. Ask yourself: am I describing one total amount? If yes, use 'is', not 'are'.

back: The news is very disturbing.
example: 'News' ends in 's' and looks plural, but it is actually an uncountable, singular noun in English - there's no such thing as 'one new' or 'two news'. Always pair 'news' with is/was, never are/were.

back: Mathematics is my favorite subject.
example: Subject names that end in '-ics', like mathematics, physics, and economics, refer to one single field of study, so they take a singular verb even though they end in 's'. Remember: the 's' at the end is just part of the word's spelling, not a plural marker here.

front: The number of students are increasing.
back: The number of students is increasing.
example: 'The number of' means one specific total, so the true subject is 'the number', which is singular. Compare this with 'a number of', which means 'many' and takes a plural verb - the word right after 'the' or 'a' makes all the difference.

front: A number of students is absent today.
back: A number of students are absent today.
example: 'A number of' means 'several' or 'many', so it behaves like a plural quantity word and needs a plural verb. Keep these two phrases straight: 'the number of' = singular, 'a number of' = plural.

back: Everybody has their own opinion.
example: Just like 'everyone', the word 'everybody' refers to a single, general person and is always grammatically singular. Pair it with 'has', not 'have', even though the meaning feels like it includes many people.

front: One of the reasons are cost.
back: One of the reasons is cost.
example: The phrase 'one of the...' always has 'one' as its true subject, no matter how many nouns follow it. Whenever you see 'one of the' at the start of a subject phrase, use a singular verb.

back: The committee has decided.
example: Committee, board, and jury describe a single official group making one decision together, so they take a singular verb in standard usage. Think of the group as one voice speaking, not many separate people.

back: Physics is a difficult subject.
example: Like mathematics, 'physics' names one academic subject, even though it ends in 's'. Words ending in -ics that describe a field of study almost always take a singular verb.

front: Neither the manager nor the staff was informed.
back: Neither the manager nor the staff were informed.
example: With 'neither...nor' and 'either...or', the verb agrees with whichever noun is closest to it - here that's 'staff', which is being used as a plural. As a rule of thumb, always look at the noun right before the verb to decide singular or plural.

front: The scissors is on the table.
back: The scissors are on the table.
example: Tools and clothing items made of two joined parts - scissors, glasses, trousers, pants - are always treated as plural in English, even though they refer to one object. There is no singular verb form for these words; always use 'are/were'.

front: Each and every student are responsible.
back: Each and every student is responsible.
example: The phrase 'each and every' emphasizes individuals one at a time, so it always takes a singular verb, just like 'each' alone does. Whenever you see 'each' anywhere before the verb, default to singular.

front: Ten years are a long time.
back: Ten years is a long time.
example: When a time period is described as one single stretch or duration, use a singular verb, even though the number is more than one. Ask: is this one block of time, or ten separate years? If it's one idea, use 'is'.

front: The police is investigating the case.
back: The police are investigating the case.
example: 'Police' is a special word in English - it has no singular form and is always treated as a plural noun, referring to police officers as a group. Always pair 'police' with are/were/have, never is/was/has.

front: Either of the answers are correct.
back: Either of the answers is correct.
example: Just like 'neither', the word 'either' means 'one of the two', so it stays singular no matter what noun follows it. Train your ear to hear 'either of' and 'neither of' as singular subjects.

front: The staff is all working overtime.
back: The staff are all working overtime.
example: When 'staff' refers to each employee doing their own separate task, rather than one unified group, English switches to a plural verb. This is different from 'staff' used as one unit - context tells you which meaning is intended.

front: Everyone in the class were present.
back: Everyone in the class was present.
example: No matter how long the phrase is between 'everyone' and the verb, 'everyone' itself is always singular. Don't let the words 'in the class' distract you - go back to the core subject, 'everyone'.

front: The jury have reached its verdict.
back: The jury has reached its verdict.
example: A jury reaching one single verdict is acting as one collective decision-making body, so it takes a singular verb. If the jury members were described as disagreeing separately, plural could be used instead - but for one shared decision, use singular.

front: Both of the answer is correct.
back: Both of the answers are correct.
example: 'Both' always refers to two things together, so it needs a plural noun (answers, not answer) and a plural verb (are). Whenever you use 'both', check that both the noun and the verb are plural.

front: There is many reasons for this.
back: There are many reasons for this.
example: In 'there is/are' sentences, the verb must match the noun that comes right after it, not the word 'there' itself. Since 'reasons' is plural, the sentence needs 'are', not 'is'.

front: Here's the keys you asked for.
back: Here are the keys you asked for.
example: "Here's" is a contraction of 'here is', which is singular, so it shouldn't be used before a plural noun like 'keys'. Before you shorten 'here is' or 'there is', check whether the noun after it is singular or plural.

front: One in five students fail the exam.
back: One in five students fails the exam.
example: In phrases like 'one in five', the actual subject is 'one', so the verb must be singular, even though 'students' appears right before the verb. Focus on the very first word of the phrase - 'one' - to decide the verb form.

front: The majority of people thinks this is fair.
back: The majority of people think this is fair.
example: 'The majority of' takes its verb form from the noun that follows it - here, 'people' is plural, so the verb must be plural too. When 'the majority of' is followed by a plural noun, always use a plural verb.

back: Economics has always interested me.
example: Like mathematics and physics, 'economics' refers to one subject area and is treated as singular, even though it ends in 's'. Don't let the final 's' fool you into thinking it needs a plural verb.

back: Neither answer is acceptable.
example: 'Neither' followed directly by a singular noun (answer, not answers) always needs a singular verb. Remember: 'neither answer is', but 'neither of the answers is' - both stay singular.

back: My glasses are broken.
example: Like scissors and trousers, 'glasses' (meaning spectacles) is always plural in English, even when referring to a single pair. Pair these two-part object words with 'are', never 'is'.

front: Everyone were invited to the party.
back: Everyone was invited to the party.
example: Even in the past tense, 'everyone' remains singular, so it must be paired with 'was', not 'were'. This rule doesn't change between tenses - 'everyone' is always singular, in any tense.

front: The group of students was arguing among themselves.
back: The group of students were arguing among themselves.
example: Here, the sentence describes the individual students disagreeing with each other separately, not the group acting as one, so the plural verb fits better. When a sentence emphasizes individual actions within a group, plural verbs are often more natural.

front: Both my sister and brother is coming.
back: Both my sister and brother are coming.
example: 'Both...and' always joins two subjects into one plural idea, so the verb must be plural. Whenever you see the pattern 'both X and Y', automatically switch to a plural verb.

front: Ten miles are too far to walk.
back: Ten miles is too far to walk.
example: A distance described as one single amount takes a singular verb, similar to money and time expressions. Ask yourself: am I talking about ten separate miles, or one total distance? Here it's one idea, so use 'is'.

front: The audience was clapping their hands.
back: The audience were clapping their hands.
example: Since each person in the audience is clapping their own hands individually, the sentence describes separate actions, which fits better with a plural verb. This is the opposite case from a group acting as one.

front: None of the food were touched.
back: None of the food was touched.
example: 'Food' is an uncountable noun, so when 'none' refers to it, the verb stays singular. Check the noun after 'of' - if it's uncountable (food, water, information), use a singular verb with 'none'.

front: None of the students was absent.
back: None of the students were absent.
example: Unlike food, 'students' is a countable plural noun, so 'none of the students' can correctly take a plural verb. With 'none of', let the noun that follows guide whether you use singular or plural.

front: The dog and the cat is playing.
back: The dog and the cat are playing.
example: When two separate subjects are joined by 'and', they combine into one plural idea, so the verb must be plural. Whenever you connect two different nouns with 'and', switch the verb to its plural form.

front: Bread and butter are my favorite breakfast.
back: Bread and butter is my favorite breakfast.
example: Even though 'and' usually creates a plural subject, when two foods are thought of as one combined dish (like bread and butter, or fish and chips), English treats the pair as singular. Ask: are these really two separate things, or one combined idea? If one idea, use singular.

back: The rich are getting richer.
example: 'The + adjective' (the rich, the poor, the elderly) is a common way to describe a whole group of people, and this pattern always takes a plural verb. Whenever 'the' is placed before an adjective to mean 'people who are ___', treat it as plural.

back: What she said was true.
example: A whole clause acting as the subject of a sentence (like 'what she said') is treated as one single idea, so it takes a singular verb. When your subject is an entire clause rather than a simple noun, default to the singular verb form.

front: Ten percent of the students is failing.
back: Ten percent of the students are failing.
example: With percentages, the verb agrees with the noun that comes after 'of' - here, 'students' is plural, so the verb must be plural. Always check the noun following 'percent of' to decide the correct verb form.

front: Ten percent of the cake are gone.
back: Ten percent of the cake is gone.
example: Here, 'cake' is an uncountable noun, so even though the number 'ten percent' looks the same as before, the verb must now be singular. The rule stays consistent: percentages match whatever noun follows 'of'.

back: The information was useful.
example: 'Information' is an uncountable noun in English - it has no plural form, so it always takes a singular verb. Never add 's' to 'information' and never use 'were' with it.

back: My advice is to wait.
example: Like 'information', 'advice' is uncountable, meaning you can't count 'one advice, two advices' - so it always stays singular. Always pair 'advice' with is/was, and never add an 's' to the word itself.

back: I know the answer.
example: 'Know' is a state verb - it describes a mental state, not an action - so it's not normally used with '-ing'. Other state verbs to remember are: like, want, need, believe, and understand. As a rule, ask yourself: is this something you're doing, or something you simply are/feel? If it's a feeling or state, avoid '-ing'.

back: I saw him yesterday.
example: The present perfect tense ('have seen') is for actions with no specific time mentioned, but 'yesterday' is a specific point in the past, so you need the simple past ('saw') instead. Whenever a sentence includes a specific past time word (yesterday, last week, in 2020), always use the simple past tense.

back: She has been living here since 2015.
example: The word 'since' signals an action that started in the past and is still continuing now, which requires the present perfect continuous tense ('has been living'), not the present continuous. Whenever you see 'since' or 'for' describing duration up to now, reach for 'have/has been + verb-ing'.

front: I am living in this city for ten years.
back: I have been living in this city for ten years.
example: Just like with 'since', the word 'for' describing a duration up to the present moment needs the present perfect continuous tense. Present continuous ('am living') only describes what's happening right now, not a duration connecting past to present.

back: He went to school yesterday.
example: 'Go' is an irregular verb, meaning its past tense doesn't simply add '-ed' - you must memorize the special form 'went'. Whenever a verb feels irregular, it's worth keeping a small personal list of these forms rather than guessing.

back: Did you go there?
example: After the helping verb 'did', the main verb must return to its base form, not stay in the past tense - 'did' already shows the sentence is in the past. A simple rule: 'did' + base verb, never 'did' + past-tense verb.

back: She didn't go to work.
example: Just like with 'did', the negative form 'didn't' already carries the past meaning, so the main verb after it must stay in its base form. Never put two past-tense markers in one verb phrase - choose either 'didn't' or the past form, not both.

back: I agree with you.
example: 'Agree' is a regular action verb, not something that needs 'am/is/are' in front of it - many learners mistakenly add 'am' out of habit from other structures. Simply use 'agree' directly after the subject, with no extra helping verb.

back: I understand your point.
example: Like 'know', 'understand' is a state verb describing a mental condition, not an ongoing action, so it doesn't take '-ing' in standard English. Whenever you notice a verb describing thinking, feeling, or possessing (understand, believe, own, belong), avoid the continuous form.

front: By the time I arrived, she left.
back: By the time I arrived, she had left.
example: When two things happened in the past, and one happened before the other, the earlier action needs the past perfect tense ('had left') to show it came first. Whenever you're describing which of two past events happened first, put 'had' before the earlier one.

front: I will call you when I will arrive.
back: I will call you when I arrive.
example: In time clauses starting with 'when', 'after', 'before', or 'as soon as', English uses the present tense to describe a future event, not 'will'. Remember: only one 'will' is needed per sentence, in the main clause - the time clause stays in present tense even though it refers to the future.

front: If it will rain, I will stay home.
back: If it rains, I will stay home.
example: Just like time clauses, 'if' clauses describing a real future possibility use the present tense, not 'will' - this is called the First Conditional. Keep the 'will' only in the result part of the sentence, never in the 'if' part.

front: I am used to wake up early.
back: I am used to waking up early.
example: The phrase 'used to' has two different meanings and forms: 'used to + base verb' describes a past habit ('I used to wake up early'), while 'be used to + -ing' describes something familiar or normal now. Since this sentence uses 'am' (present), it needs the -ing form: 'used to waking up'.

back: I have a car.
example: When 'have' means possession (owning something), it's a state verb and doesn't take the continuous form. 'Having' is only correct when it means 'experiencing' something, like 'I am having a great time' - possession itself should stay simple.

front: She has gone to market yesterday.
back: She went to the market yesterday.
example: Just like the earlier mistake with 'seen', specific past time words like 'yesterday' require the simple past tense, not the present perfect. If you can name exactly when something happened, use simple past, not 'has/have + past participle'.

front: I am not knowing what to do.
back: I don't know what to do.
example: Since 'know' is a state verb, it should never appear in a continuous ('-ing') form, even in the negative. The correct negative pattern for state verbs is simply 'don't/doesn't + base verb'.

back: He has two brothers.
example: Describing family relationships or possessions with 'have' is a state, not an action, so no '-ing' form is used. This exact mistake is extremely common, so it's worth practicing 'has two brothers/sisters' as a fixed, memorized phrase.

front: I will have finished by tomorrow, I think.
back: I will have finished by tomorrow.
example: This sentence is grammatically fine, but a very common related error is dropping 'have' entirely, saying 'I will finished' - always keep the full structure 'will have + past participle' together as one unit for actions completed before a future point.

back: I like this song.
example: 'Like' is a state verb describing a feeling, not an ongoing action, so standard English avoids the continuous form (some informal marketing slogans use it for effect, but it's not grammatically standard). Stick with simple present for feelings: like, love, hate, prefer.

front: When I was young, I use to play outside.
back: When I was young, I used to play outside.
example: 'Used to' (with a 'd') describes a past habit that no longer happens - many learners drop the 'd' when writing because it sounds the same when spoken. Always remember the 'd' in writing: u-s-e-d to.

back: I have finished my homework.
example: The present perfect tense needs the past participle form of the verb ('finished'), not the base form ('finish'). Always double-check that the verb after 'have/has' is in its correct past participle form.

back: She has written three books.
example: 'Write' is irregular, and its past participle is 'written', not 'wrote' (which is the simple past form). It helps to memorize irregular verbs in three forms together: write, wrote, written.

back: I have seen that movie already.
example: 'Seen' is the past participle of 'see' and can never be used alone as a main verb - it always needs a helping verb like 'have' or 'has' before it. Whenever you use a past participle (seen, done, gone, written), check that 'have/has/had' comes before it.

back: I have done my work.
example: The past participle of 'do' is 'done', not 'did' (which is the simple past tense form). Keep the three forms straight: do, did, done - 'done' always follows 'have/has/had'.

back: He has eaten his lunch.
example: The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten', not 'ate'. This follows the same pattern as 'write/wrote/written' - many irregular verbs have three completely different forms to memorize.

front: I was knowing him for years before we met properly.
back: I had known him for years before we met properly.
example: Since 'know' is a state verb, it should never appear in a continuous form - and because this describes something true before another past event, it needs the past perfect: 'had known'. Combine both rules here: no continuous form for state verbs, and past perfect for the earlier of two past events.

front: She is working here since five years.
back: She has been working here for five years.
example: Two things need fixing here: use present perfect continuous ('has been working') instead of present continuous, and use 'for' (not 'since') before a length of time - 'since' is only used with a starting point, like 'since 2018'. Remember: 'for' + duration, 'since' + starting point.

front: I am waiting here since morning.
back: I have been waiting here since morning.
example: As before, this needs present perfect continuous because the waiting started in the past and continues now, and 'since morning' correctly marks a starting point, matching the word 'since'. The mistake here is only in the verb tense, not the preposition.

back: I didn't see him today.
example: Just like with 'did', the negative 'didn't' already shows past tense, so the verb after it must be in its base form, not past tense. Watch for this exact pattern - 'didn't' + base verb - since it's one of the most frequent tense mistakes.

front: Yesterday, I am going to the market.
back: Yesterday, I went to the market.
example: The word 'yesterday' clearly signals the simple past tense, so mixing it with a present tense verb creates a confusing, incorrect sentence. Always match your tense to your time word - past time words need past tense verbs.

front: Tomorrow, I went to the market.
back: Tomorrow, I will go to the market.
example: Just as 'yesterday' needs past tense, 'tomorrow' needs future tense ('will go'), since it refers to a time that hasn't happened yet. Get in the habit of checking your time word first, then choosing the matching tense.

front: I have gone there last week.
back: I went there last week.
example: Once again, 'last week' is a specific past time marker, so this sentence needs the simple past tense, not the present perfect. If you can point to exactly when something happened, avoid using 'have/has' with it.

front: I am doing this since morning.
back: I have been doing this since morning.
example: The word 'since' always signals an action continuing from the past into now, which requires present perfect continuous, not present continuous alone. This is one of the most frequent patterns learners get wrong, so it's worth practicing this exact sentence type.

front: She said she is coming tomorrow.
back: She said she was coming tomorrow.
example: When reporting what someone said in the past ('she said'), the tense of the reported part usually shifts back one step too - present becomes past. This pattern is called 'backshifting' in reported speech, and it applies even when the original statement was about the future.

front: He told me he will help me.
back: He told me he would help me.
example: Just like other reported speech, 'will' shifts back to 'would' when reporting what someone said in the past. Whenever the reporting verb ('said', 'told') is in the past, shift the tense of the quoted part back by one step.

front: I hope you will enjoyed the party.
back: I hope you will enjoy the party.
example: After 'will', the verb must stay in its base form ('enjoy'), never in the past tense ('enjoyed'). This mistake often happens when a sentence has multiple verbs and the writer loses track of which form belongs where.

back: She has already gone home.
example: The correct past participle of 'go' is 'gone', not 'went' - remember the three forms: go, went, gone. 'Went' can only be used alone as a simple past tense verb, never after 'have/has/had'.

front: I would went if I had time.
back: I would go if I had time.
example: After 'would', the main verb must be in its base form, not the past tense - this is the same rule as after 'will'. Keep this consistent across all modal verbs (will, would, can, could, should): always base form afterward.

front: If I was rich, I will buy a house.
back: If I were rich, I would buy a house.
example: This is a Second Conditional sentence describing an unreal, hypothetical present situation, which needs 'were' (not 'was') in the if-clause and 'would' (not 'will') in the result. Learn this whole pattern as a set: If + past tense (were), would + base verb.

back: I wish I were taller.
example: After 'wish' when talking about something untrue right now, English uses the past tense form 'were' (even with 'I'), showing the wish is unreal or contrary to fact. This pattern feels unusual at first, but it's a fixed structure worth memorizing: wish + past tense, for present wishes.

front: I wish I will pass the exam.
back: I wish I would pass the exam.
